user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: the spread has now broken decisively below 0.70, hitting a new 12-month low of 0.61, exactly the falsifier condition I set for renewed flattening. The last three readings (0.63, 0.63, 0.61) show flattening momentum has not stalled, with slope_change_21d at -0.18pp and slope_change_63d at -0.46pp both confirming a persistent, accelerating flattening trend rather than noise. The spread sits at slope_range_position 0.00 — the very bottom of its 12-month range — with slope_distance_from_flat down to just 0.61pp, meaning inversion risk is now much closer. Policy language has shifted hawkish: guidance moved from tapering-in-progress to "soon appropriate to raise the target range," which historically compresses front-end-to-belly spreads faster than the long end, consistent with the observed flattening. The 10s30s slope at +0.32pp remains modestly positive, suggesting the flattening pressure is concentrated in the 2s10s segment rather than a broad long-end selloff. With no signs of stabilization in the last three prints and hawkish rhetoric intensifying, I expect continued flattening into month-end, though the pace of decline may moderate somewhat given how compressed the spread already is.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.70 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_10s30s

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially stabilized after its earlier tightening drift. Over the last 13 weeks the index rose modestly (+0.08) from very loose levels, but the most recent 4 weeks show a slight reversal (-0.01), with the last several prints clustered tightly between -0.54 and -0.56. The latest value (-0.55) sits right at the middle of this recent range, and the range_position of 0.90 shows we're near the high (tighter) end of the 52-week span, but momentum has flattened rather than continuing to build. My prior flat call was directionally correct — the anticipated resumption of a clear multi-week rise did not materialize, nor did a drop back toward -0.60. The series is oscillating in a narrow band with no clear trend. Policy language remains unchanged, describing conditions as "accommodative," consistent with the very negative distance-from-zero reading (-0.55), but this is stale boilerplate and not informative for the one-month change. Given the flattening momentum and lack of a decisive directional push in either the measurements or the policy text, I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than before since the range has now held for several consecutive weeks without breaking.
Would change its mind if: A break below -0.60 (renewed easing) or a sustained multi-week climb toward -0.50 or higher (renewed tightening) would change this call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakevens (2.46→2.44) show stabilization after an early dip, not a renewed uptrend. The series bottomed near 2.33 mid-window, rebounded to 2.38-2.44, and has now printed flat for two consecutive readings (2.44, 2.44). My prior "up" call was wrong in direction of magnitude — the 21d and 63d changes are both modestly negative (-0.14, -0.13 pp), showing the medium-term trend is still net down from where it was a month/quarter ago, even though the most recent few prints have leveled off. The 252d change (+0.31pp) shows expectations remain well above where they were a year ago, and the range position (0.51) sits right in the middle of the 12-month band, arguing against a strong directional signal either way. Distance from target remains firmly above 2% (+0.44pp), consistent with expectations anchored moderately above objective, but that's a level story, not a momentum one. Given the flattening in the last two prints after the dip, and negative but decelerating short/medium-term momentum, I don't see strong evidence for continued upward drift. The most defensible call now is essentially flat, with a slight lean toward stabilization rather than a resumed rise.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.50 or below 2.35 sustained over multiple observations would confirm a genuine directional move rather than range-bound flatness.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.62
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose further to 7.56% from 7.17%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.21% from 4.89%, extending an unbroken multi-month acceleration. The falsifier I set (two consecutive sub-0.3% mom prints plus core PCE turning down) did not occur — mom actually reaccelerated: 0.86 → 0.69 → 0.60, still well above the 0.72% 3-month average and far above any level consistent with 2% annualized inflation. The 3m annualized headline pace (8.95%) is running hotter than the 12m y/y rate (7.56%), and core 3m annualized (6.64%) likewise exceeds its own y/y print, meaning momentum is not decelerating even though the single latest mom reading (0.60) is modestly below the immediately preceding two. Policy language confirms this isn't yet seen as easing: officials shifted from calling imbalances "transitory" to acknowledging inflation "has exceeded 2 percent for some time," and began tapering asset purchases — a clear acknowledgment of sustained overheating. Core is running far above target (+3.21pp) and the range position is at the top of its 12-month band (1.00). While the deceleration in mom from 0.86 to 0.60 is a mild yellow flag worth watching, it's not enough on its own to break the trend given still-elevated 3m annualized rates and rising y/y in both series. I still expect headline CPI y/y to rise next month.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the 12m y/y rate.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, yoy_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.00% from 3.90%, an uptick of 0.10pp after a long, steep decline (-2.40pp over 12 months). This confirms my prior falsifier condition — I said a print at or above 4.00-4.10% would signal stalling, and that's exactly what happened. The unemployment rate is now essentially at the 3m average (4.00%) and just 0.10pp above the 12-month low, so the Sahm-gap remains tiny (+0.10pp) and doesn't yet indicate a genuine trend reversal or rapid deterioration. However, the multi-month streak of consecutive declines has clearly broken — this is the first uptick after six straight prints of falling or flat readings. Policy language still describes "substantial" declines and "solid" job gains, but that language reflects the trend through the prior prints, not this newest uptick, and is now being used to justify tightening (tapering asset purchases), consistent with labor conditions being assessed as near maximum employment rather than still loosening rapidly. Given the level is at the natural rate (distance_from_natural ≈ 0) and the increase is small but real, I read this as a stalling of the tightening trend rather than a resumption of loosening — a flat-to-slightly-up call with modest conviction, not a confident reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.20% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.50pp would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than a stall near the natural rate.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated: dgs10 has climbed from 1.75 to 1.79 recently after a dip, with 21d change +0.27pp and 63d +0.22pp, both positive. The 12-month range position sits at 0.90, meaning the yield is near the top of its trailing range, and it's 0.70pp above its 12m low versus only 0.08pp below the 12m high. The 252d change of +0.68pp confirms a strong sustained uptrend over the past year. The 10s30s slope of +0.32pp is not inverted, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening from flight-to-quality. Policy language confirms continued tapering of Treasury purchases (now down to $20bn/month from $40bn), which reduces central bank demand for long-end paper and should keep upward pressure on term premium via reduced balance-sheet support. This is a incremental continuation of the previously announced taper, not a reversal, so it reinforces rather than changes the up call. Momentum has been choppy short-term (1.85→1.81→1.78→1.79) but the broader trend and level near range highs support continued modest upward pressure into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-session drop below 1.70 with negative 21d momentum would signal easing term-premium pressure and reverse this call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) was correct in direction: fed_assets rose again to 8,860,485 from 8,867,834 one observation back — actually the latest print (8,860,485) is slightly below the prior observation (8,867,834), a small dip, but the broader 4-week and 13-week changes remain strongly positive (+103,025 and +304,304 respectively), and the level sits essentially at the 52-week high (only 7,349 below it). The policy language confirms continued net asset purchases, but at a tapering pace: Treasury purchases cut from $80B/month equivalent path down to $20B/month and MBS to $10B/month beginning [date] following a prior cut from $40B/$20B. This is still QE — assets are still being added, not drained — but the pace of expansion is decelerating sharply toward zero. Given tapering is explicit and nearly complete, the balance sheet is still growing but at a much slower rate, and the single most recent observation ticked down slightly, which could be noise or the first sign of the taper's effect. Net acquisition pace is small and shrinking, consistent with expansion continuing through month-end but at a diminishing rate, not yet flat or reversing.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing purchases reduced to zero/runoff, would flip this to flat or down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  labor_tightness lacks balance_sheet — Pace and composition of future runoff/taper decisions directly drive long-end supply pressure.
  labor_tightness lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could trigger flight-to-quality that would offset taper-driven upward pressure.

arbitrated.notes:
This panel mislabels its seats relative to my mandate, so I map to what each is actually measuring. "labor_tightness" here is actually reporting DGS10 level/momentum (a term-premium/duration proxy, not unemployment), while "inflation" reports unemployment rate (labor market slack) and "inflation_expectations" reports actual CPI/PCE. "curve_slope" reports NFCI (financial conditions) and "financial_conditions" reports breakevens (inflation expectations). I read the underlying content, not the seat label, since the trade is about yield level.

Content-wise: realized inflation (CPI 7.56%, core PCE 5.21%, still accelerating) is the freshest and most decisive signal — clearly up, and policy has already shifted hawkish (tapering, "exceeded 2% for some time"). The unemployment print ticked up slightly (3.90→4.00) but this is a tiny, ambiguous move near the natural rate — not a strong loosening signal, and stale-ish (16 days). Breakevens have flattened after a dip — no strong directional pull either way. NFCI has stabilized in a narrow band — also flat. The DGS10-proxy driver ("labor_tightness" seat) shows continued modest uptrend near 12-month range highs, driven by taper reducing central-bank long-end demand — consistent with rising term premium. Balance sheet growth is still positive but decelerating sharply toward zero, which itself removes a support for lower yields (i.e., points toward higher term premium/duration risk going forward), even though the balance-sheet driver's own measurement (asset level) is still rising in the near term — so I keep that framed as its own direction (up) while noting the yield implication is upward pressure via taper.

Net: strong, fresh, accelerating inflation prints plus taper-driven reduction in duration demand (term premium up) dominate a flat/ambiguous financial-conditions and labor picture. I raise conviction modestly from a presumed prior flat/mild-up stance toward a clearer up call on yield level, keeping the position long-duration-short (higher yields), maintained across both legs.

discounted.why: Ambiguous, stale unemployment uptick near natural rate; too small to signal genuine loosening or tightening.
Breakevens range-bound after dip; no clear directional pull on inflation expectations or yields.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI/core PCE deceleration (two sub-0.3% mom prints, core PCE y/y turning down) combined with fed_assets outright declining or DGS10 breaking below 1.70 sustained.

risks.text: A sudden dovish pivot if inflation data surprises down would reverse the taper-driven term-premium thesis quickly.
Consensus long-yield positioning could unwind sharply on any growth scare, causing a flight-to-quality rally.
Balance sheet still expanding, just slower — if taper pace stalls or reverses, term premium support fades.

trade.rationale: Accelerating inflation and taper-driven reduction in long-end demand both push yields higher; maintain a modestly larger long-end tilt given term-premium driver, same-sign level bet.

why.inflation: CPI and core PCE both accelerating, 3m annualized exceeds y/y, freshest and most decisive read of realized inflation.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment ticked up marginally (3.90→4.00) near natural rate; weak, stale, ambiguous signal on labor slack, not a strong loosening call.
why.term_premium: DGS10 near 12m range highs, positive 21d/63d/252d momentum; taper removes long-end demand, pushing term premium up.
why.financial_conditions: Breakevens flattened after dip, range-bound mid-band; no strong directional signal, slight lean toward stabilization.
why.balance_sheet: Fed assets still rising (own measurement up) but growth decelerating sharply toward zero as taper completes.

Call: down, conviction 0.60

All momentum measures confirm this: 21d change -0.24, 63d change -0.59, 252d change -0.91, all negative and accelerating over longer windows. The policy language has shifted decisively hawkish — from tapering to an explicit signal that a hike is imminent ("expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range"), with asset purchases ending in early [date] . This front-loads tightening expectations, pressuring the front end (2Y) higher relative to the long end, which is the classic mechanism for flattening. The 10s30s slope at 0.34 shows the long end itself is not steepening either, consistent with a broad-based flattening rather than a front-end-only phenomenon. With the spread already pinned near cycle lows and hawkish rhetoric intensifying rather than stabilizing, there is no evidence of a rebound — my stated falsifier (sustained readings above 0.70, or 21d turning positive) has not been met. I expect continued modest flattening or stabilization at a compressed level.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.55 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled or reversed.

Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has moved from -0.55 to -0.47 over the last several weeks, a clear and steady rise. The 4-week change (+0.09) and 13-week change (+0.10) are consistent with each other, indicating this is not noise but a sustained trend of tightening momentum. The index is now sitting at its 52-week high, with range_position at 1.00 - the top of its recent band - confirming this is the tightest reading in the observed window, even though the level (-0.47) remains below the zero average, so conditions are still net accommodative in absolute terms. My prior flat call was wrong: at that point the climb toward -0.50 was already underway and has now cleared through that threshold, which I had flagged as my trigger to reverse. The steady, monotonic [date] upward over the last several observations (particularly the last 4: -0.54, -0.52, -0.49, -0.47) shows accelerating tightening momentum rather than stabilization. Policy language remains static and accommodative-oriented, but the measurement itself is the graded signal and it points unambiguously higher. I now call this up with good conviction given the consistency across both short and medium windows.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.52 or flattening over 2+ weeks would suggest the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been invalidated: breakeven_10y broke decisively above 2.50 and has continued climbing to 2.62, the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.77, only 0.14pp below the 12m high of 2.76). The last two readings (2.54→2.62) show acceleration, not consolidation, and the 21-day change (+0.22pp) is now much larger than the 63-day change (+0.07pp), indicating the move is recent and gaining pace rather than fading. The 252-day change (+0.47pp) confirms a sustained uptrend over the year, not noise. Distance from the 2% objective is now +0.62pp, well above where it sat a few observations ago, suggesting market-implied inflation expectations are becoming increasingly unanchored to the upside relative to target. With price action pushing to fresh highs within the range and momentum building rather than decelerating, the evidence supports continuation over reversion. I was wrong to stay flat last time — the multi-observation break above 2.50 that I set as my falsifier has now occurred emphatically. I'll shift to a clear "up" call, tempered only by the fact that breakevens are near the top of their historical range and could see some mean-reversion resistance there.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below 2.50 sustained over 2-3 observations, or a flattening of the 21-day change toward zero, would signal the uptrend has stalled.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.68
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI rose from 7.56% to 7.94% y/y, and every underlying measure confirms continued acceleration rather than any turn. Core PCE climbed again to 5.36% from 5.21%, still rising monotonically for 13 straight readings with no sign of plateauing. The 3m annualized rates for both headline (8.25%) and core (6.65%) remain well above the trailing y/y rates, meaning recent momentum is running hotter than the reported year-over-year figure — a strong signal that the y/y print itself has further to climb before base effects catch up. The outgoing MoM print (0.52%) is below the 3-month average (0.66%), but at 0.52% it's still a very hot monthly pace in absolute terms, nowhere near the sub-0.3% threshold that would have flagged deceleration. Policy language shifted meaningfully: the Committee moved from "inflation having exceeded 2 percent for some time" tapering language to explicitly "inflation well above 2 percent" with tightening now imminent — confirming officials see continued overheating, not moderation. Core-headline gap remains wide but core is still accelerating, which is the more persistent signal. All evidence — momentum, level, and policy characterization — points the same direction with no offsetting signal.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the trailing y/y rate.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 3.90% from 4.00%, matching the 12-month low and putting the unemployment rate at the bottom of its 13-month range (range position 0.00). The Sahm gap is essentially nil (+0.03pp), confirming no loosening signal whatsoever. The 3m change (-0.20pp) and 6m change (-1.20pp) both show continued tightening, not stabilization. My prior "flat" call was proven wrong by this data — the market kept tightening rather than stalling near the natural rate. The level is now 0.10pp below my assumed natural rate anchor of 4%, indicating a historically tight labor market with no sign of turning. Policy language also confirms this: statements shifted from acknowledging "further improvement in the labor market" to declaring a "strong labor market" as justification for raising rates soon — language consistent with a labor market at or beyond full employment, not one showing cracks. Momentum has been consistently downward (declining unemployment) for months. Absent a clear reversal signal, the most likely near-term outcome is continued flatness at this very low level, with a modest risk of a further tick down given the persistent trend, rather than any reversal upward.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.10% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.30pp would signal a genuine turn toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:

My prior "up" call was directionally right on the medium-term trend but the most recent data point is a pullback, not a continuation. Still, 21d change is only +0.02pp — essentially flat over the past month — so the single-day drop hasn't yet produced sustained negative momentum. The yield sits at 0.74 of its 12-month range (well above the low), still much closer to the top than the bottom, and the 10s30s slope remains a healthy +0.34pp, consistent with ongoing term-premium richness rather than collapse. Policy language continues to taper asset purchases (further $20bn/$10bn reduction beginning in the new month), which reduces official-sector demand for long-end paper and structurally supports higher term premium over time, even though the latest print dipped. Given the tapering continues and the level remains high in its range, I lean toward the drift resuming upward over the next month, but the recent dip and flat 21d change temper confidence. This is a lean, not a strong call.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-session move below 1.75 with negative 21d momentum and flattening 30s10s slope would indicate the term-premium uptrend has genuinely reversed.

This is still net QE, just at a shrinking rate. With purchases still positive but being halved again, assets should keep rising into month-end, but by a smaller increment than the trailing 4-week pace suggests, since the new lower monthly pace only partially reflects in the data shown. No language yet signals a move to zero purchases or runoff, so I expect continued (slowing) growth in assets, not a reversal. Reserve creation continues, just at a decelerating clip, which is the "why" behind the still-positive but flattening curve of increases.
Would change its mind if: A month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing an end to purchases/shift to runoff, would flip this to flat or down.

With no evidence of momentum reversal, deceleration, or claims data suggesting a inflection, I lean toward continued tightness. A single month at a fresh cyclical low is not a guarantee of further decline, but there is no basis to expect a reversal either — flat-to-down is the best supported call.

arbitrated.notes: The panel this month is a mislabeled version of last time's — the analyst names have shifted (curve_slope, inflation_expectations, labor_tightness/inflation swapped roles) but the substance maps cleanly onto the five drivers I track. Inflation: the inflation_expectations read (headline CPI 8.57%, core PCE 5.60%, both accelerating, 3m annualized above y/y on both measures) is a clean, fresh, high-conviction acceleration signal — stronger than last meeting, not weaker. The "inflation" entry (unemployment-based) is really labor tightness mislabeled: UNRATE at 3.70%, fresh 12-month low, Sahm gap tame — this is a tightening labor market, i.e., labor_tightness should be read as tightening (falling unemployment = tighter, per the inversion note), not "down." Labor_tightness's own entry (DGS10-based term-premium proxy, momentum still positive, balance-sheet runoff language reinforcing) I read as term_premium given its content (dgs10 levels/slope) — up, modestly. Financial_conditions gave a "down" call on breakevens rolling over three prints, a genuine reversal from last meeting's "up" — this is a real change, not staleness, so I dock financial_conditions materially versus last time. Curve_slope's NFCI read (accommodative-but-tightening, monotonic climb) is actually financial_conditions content — tightening, reinforcing hawkish pressure. Balance_sheet: spread collapsing toward inversion is curve-shape (out of scope for me), but the underlying policy language — active hiking, balance-sheet runoff coming — is unambiguously hawkish for the level. Net: inflation is accelerating and labor market is tightening, both push yields up; financial conditions readings are mixed (NFCI tightening supports up, breakevens rolling over is a modest pushback); balance-sheet runoff guidance is confirmed and hawkish. This still nets to higher yields, though the breakeven reversal and curve flattening toward inversion are genuine new frictions versus last meeting, warranting a slight resizing rather than a reversal.

Inflation is now split across two proxies: headline CPI (inflation_expectations analyst) jumped to 8.54%/0.95% MoM — clearly hot and accelerating — while the separate "inflation" read (unemployment-based, mislabeled but tracking labor slack) hit a fresh 12-month low, confirming tightness. I weight the fresher, more directly relevant headline CPI reading over the stale core signal. Labor_tightness (here proxied by DGS10-level features, an odd construction) confirms uptrend with high conviction, reinforcing higher long yields directly. Financial_conditions is genuinely mixed: the NFCI-style curve_slope read shows tightening financial conditions unambiguously and with rising conviction (0.55, accelerating trend, all data confirms), while the breakeven-based financial_conditions call is a tepid "up" (0.30) off a much lower base — I lean on the higher-conviction, monotonic NFCI trend. Balance_sheet flipped from down to a tentative up (steepening lean) but conviction is low and mixed (long-term trend still deeply negative); I treat this as weak and largely offsetting against the term_premium analyst's read, which says balance-sheet runoff is now confirmed starting, assets falling sharply — a hawkish supply-side, term-premium-widening signal with high conviction (0.65). Falsifier conditions from last time (breakevens sustained >2.95, unemployment >3.90%) have not triggered — if anything the opposite. I hold the long-duration-up position, modestly increasing conviction given corroboration across financial conditions, inflation, and confirmed balance-sheet runoff, tempered by disagreement on the balance_sheet reversal signal and core PCE plateau.

This panel has genuinely shifted, and the labels have gotten confusing — I need to reconcile carefully. There appear to be two analysts covering financial-conditions-type ground (curve_slope, reporting NFCI tightening, and financial_conditions, reporting breakeven collapse) plus a duplicate on term_premium (fed balance sheet: labor_tightness reads DGS10 level/momentum, term_premium reads fed_assets, balance_sheet reads 2s10s slope). Taking the driver names at face value per the schema: balance_sheet (2s10s spread) has flipped from up to down, sharply — a fresh, decisive reversal with strong recent momentum (-0.26pp/21d) that I must respect. term_premium (fed assets) is a weak up on a technical 4-week bounce against a still-negative 13-week trend — low conviction, borderline noise. financial_conditions (breakevens) has flipped hard from up to down, with three consecutive accelerating declines well past its own falsifier — this is the single freshest, most decisive read on the panel. labor_tightness (DGS10 momentum) still leans up but flags its own reversal risk with reduced conviction, and is 22 days stale. inflation (unemployment) stays down/tight at reduced 0.30 conviction, 15 days stale, showing deceleration near a floor.

Netting these for the level view: falling breakevens is a real disinflationary signal pulling nominal yields down; the 2s10s flattening reversal also argues front-end-driven compression, not a broad steepening-up move. Against that, labor market tightness and modestly elevated headline CPI still argue for some upward pressure, but both are staler and lower-conviction than the fresh breakeven and curve reversals. My prior falsifier condition (breakeven reversal below 2.85 + unemployment reversal + NFCI reversing) is partially met — breakevens have broken decisively lower — even though unemployment hasn't turned and NFCI (per curve_slope) is tightening, not reversing. This is a genuine partial-flip: enough to trim my long-duration-short-yields stance, not enough to go flat or reverse to bullish duration.

arbitrated.notes:
Naming conventions here are confusing: the driver labeled "labor_tightness" in this panel is actually reporting on DGS10 level/momentum (a term-premium/duration-supply proxy), while "inflation" reports on unemployment rate. I'll take each analyst's own content at face value regardless of label mismatch versus my seat's usual mapping.

Relative to last meeting, the picture has shifted moderately but not decisively. Balance sheet: still deeply inverted 2s10s spread, medium/long trend flattening, but 21d momentum has stalled — this is deceleration of the flattening call, not a reversal. I move it toward flat from a clear negative. Term premium (fed balance sheet total assets) confirms continued runoff, unchanged policy language, no rebound above 13w average — my own falsifier didn't trigger, so I hold this down/contracting call firmly. Financial conditions (breakevens) flipped: prior "up" call was wrong, now breakevens have broken down below 2.50, a genuine trend reversal, so down/falling breakevens is now a live, fresh read — inflation expectations cooling. Inflation (CPI/PCE) confirms continued deceleration in headline, though core stickier. Labor market (unemployment proxy) ticked up slightly but stayed in-band — flat, low conviction, not a strong loosening signal yet. The DGS10-momentum analyst (mislabeled labor_tightness) shows continued climb in yields/range-position, own-flip to "up" — but this is a rate-outcome measure, not a fresh fundamental driver, and creates circularity risk if I lean on it for a yield-level call.

Net: disinflationary pull (breakevens + CPI) and continued balance-sheet runoff (upward pressure on term premium/yields via reduced Fed demand) are pulling in opposite directions on the level, while curve/labor signals are muted-to-flat. Financial conditions loosening modestly (NFCI flattened) is a mild offsetting force too. This is a genuinely split panel with no single driver decisively re-anchoring the level from where I sat last month. I hold my small long-duration lean but trim conviction slightly given the increased disagreement and the confirmed-but-decelerating balance sheet flattening, while modestly weighting the fresher breakeven reversal and confirmed runoff as still net supportive of a slightly higher-yield, roughly-flat stance rather than a strong directional call either way.

  You called: balance_sheet -0.20, financial_conditions -0.55, inflation -0.35, labor_tightness +0.10, term_premium -0.65
  The position you are carrying: DGS10 +0.10, DGS2 +0.08 (conviction 0.18).
  You said this read would flip if: A sustained breakeven rebound above 2.55 for 2+ prints, or Fed assets rebounding above 13w average, would flip toward a firmer up-in-yields call; a 2s10s break above -0.15 with positive 21d momentum would argue for steepening-driven higher long yields specifically.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread remains deeply inverted near -0.39pp, essentially unchanged from the last print (-0.40) and within the recent -0.33 to -0.51 range. My prior "flat" call held up: the spread has oscillated in a narrow band for the last several readings without a clear directional break. slope_change_21d is modestly negative (-0.09pp), showing no near-term steepening, while the 63d (-0.43) and 252d (-1.62) changes confirm the broader flattening/inversion trend is still dominant on longer lookbacks — the curve has moved much further inverted over the past year than any recent bounce would suggest reversing. slope_range_position at 0.07 shows the spread sits near the bottom of its 12-month range, reinforcing that we are still in deep inversion territory, not a recovery. The Fed's continued rate hikes (fed funds raised to 3-3.25%, with more increases anticipated) keep front-end yields (dgs2 +4.22%) pinned up relative to the long end (dgs10 +3.83%), which is consistent with continued curve inversion pressure rather than steepening. The 10s30s slope (-0.04) shows the long end itself is nearly flat, adding no offsetting steepening impulse. Absent a clear break higher, I expect the spread to stay range-bound near current inverted levels into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break above -0.15pp with slope_change_21d turning consistently positive (>0.10pp) would confirm a genuine steepening reversal rather than continued range-bound inversion.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, dgs2, dgs10, slope_10s30s

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  NFCI has risen steadily from -0.24 to -0.12 over the last several weeks, with the 4-week change now +0.10 and the reading sitting at the top of its 52-week range (range_position = 1.00, at the 52w high of -0.12). This confirms the tightening trend I flagged as a threshold last time: my prior "flat" call was wrong given the data — the series has moved consistently in one direction (7 of the last 8 observations show sequential increases), not oscillating around a level. Conditions remain looser than the historical zero average (distance_from_zero -0.12), but the trajectory is unambiguously toward tighter conditions, having erased more than half the distance from the 52w low (-0.65) to zero. Momentum is consistent across both the 4-week and 13-week windows (+0.10 and +0.05 respectively), with the shorter window accelerating, suggesting the move is not stalling. Absent any offsetting signal, I expect the index to continue drifting higher (tighter) over the next month, likely testing or breaking through the zero line if the pace holds. I am upgrading conviction from my prior flat call given the clear, sustained directional momentum and the series now printing new 52-week highs.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.18 over the next few weekly prints, or a flattening of the 4-week change toward zero, would undercut this tightening call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.55) was correct: breakeven_10y fell further to 2.15 from 2.19, extending the decline rather than rebounding. The series has now dropped sharply over the last two prints (2.33→2.19→2.15), with be_change_21d at -0.33pp showing the pace of decline is accelerating relative to the 63d (-0.19pp) and 252d (-0.23pp) windows — a clear negative momentum signal, not just a slow drift. The series sits at its 12-month low (be_above_12m_low = 0.00pp, be_range_position = 0.00), meaning there is no technical support nearby from recent history; the market is pricing weaker forward inflation than at any point in the past year. Distance from the 2% target has compressed to +0.15pp, the smallest cushion in this window, suggesting expectations are converging toward or through target rather than running hot. With no rebound evidence and acceleration in the short-term change, I expect the breakeven to continue drifting lower or at best stabilize near this new low over the coming month. I don't see technical or narrative support for a reversal yet, though the pace of decline may decelerate given how compressed the level already is versus target.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.30 sustained for 2+ observations would signal the decline has reversed rather than continuing.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_above_12m_low, be_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  Last release: my prior "down" call was wrong. Headline y/y ticked up 8.19 vs 8.22 prior — essentially flat, not the decline I expected, and the mom print (0.42) came in above the 3m average, undercutting the disinflation thesis. Core PCE y/y actually rose to 5.36 from 5.11, back near its cycle high and above my own flip threshold (5.3%). The 3-month annualized core pace (5.65%) is running hotter than the y/y rate itself, signaling reacceleration in underlying momentum, not just base effects. Headline's 3m annualized (1.90%) looks tame, but that gap between core and headline (-2.83pp) reflects energy/food swings that are not persistent — core is the better signal here given the divergence, and core is clearly moving the wrong way. Headline y/y is still elevated in the upper part of its 12m range (0.71 position) and the deceleration from the 8.98 peak has stalled over the last three prints (8.46, 8.22, 8.19) rather than continuing to fall. Given core reacceleration and stalling headline disinflation, I now lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call for next month's headline print rather than continued decline.
Would change its mind if: A clear drop in headline MoM back below 0.2% alongside core PCE y/y falling back under 5.2% would restore the disinflation case.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, headline_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.50% matches the 12-month low, confirming my prior "flat" call was directionally correct—the market did not loosen further, and if anything ticked tighter than the prior reading of 3.60%. Unemployment is now 0.50pp below the natural-rate anchor of 4%, and the trend over 3/6/12 months remains negative (-0.10, -0.20, -1.20 pp respectively), indicating a still-tight labor market with no loosening signal. The Sahm gap is a negligible +0.03pp, far from the ~0.5pp threshold that would flag a recessionary turn. Range position at 0.00 shows the rate sitting exactly at its trailing-year floor, the opposite of a Sahm-style rapid-rise signal. Policy language reinforces this: "job gains have been robust... unemployment rate has remained low," with the added "Nonetheless" suggesting the Committee is calling out labor strength as notable or perhaps in tension with other conditions, but not describing any loosening emerging in labor markets themselves. Given the rate is at a fresh low with no upward momentum, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-tight next month, expecting the unemployment rate to hold near 3.5% or tick marginally within noise. There is no evidence here of turning higher.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a turning point toward loosening; a print at or below 3.50% would suggest tightening is still active.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_low, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct in direction: dgs10 rose again to 3.83, extending the trend from 3.49 ten observations ago. However, the most recent print shows the 10y pulled back from a 3.97 peak to 3.72-3.83, so momentum has moderated even though the level remains near the top of its range. Range position at 0.95 confirms the yield is sitting near its 12-month high, and the 63d/252d changes (+0.95pp, +2.29pp) show a strong, persistent uptrend. The 30s10s slope is only -0.04pp, essentially flat, which doesn't show the kind of curve inversion at the long end that would flag an exhaustion of the term-premium rise. Continued balance-sheet runoff language remains a structural tailwind for higher long-end yields via increased net supply absorption. That said, the pullback from 3.97 to 3.72 before the latest tick back up to 3.83 introduces some two-sided risk — the move is decelerating even if not reversing. I'll maintain an "up" call but temper conviction given the loss of momentum near cycle highs and the risk of consolidation at these elevated levels.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward 3.50 with negative 21d momentum and a flattening/inverting 30s10s would signal the upward push has genuinely stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: total assets fell again to 8,795,567 from 8,816,802, continuing an uninterrupted decline. Over the last 4 weeks assets are down $30,526M, over 13 weeks down $117,986M (-1.32%), and the series sits well below its 52w average and $169,920M below the 52w high. The 4w pace (~$30.5B) is roughly consistent with, if not slightly faster than, the recent 13w average monthly pace (~$117,986/3 ≈ $39,329M per 4-week period, so actually the most recent 4w reading is a bit slower than the 13w trend, but still firmly negative). Policy language remains unchanged, explicitly confirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per the announced runoff plan — no signal of tapering or pausing QT. There is no evidence of reinvestment resumption or balance-sheet growth in any recent print; every one of the last several weekly observations is lower than the one before it, a clean monotonic decline. Given the unchanged cap-driven runoff language and the consistent multi-week downward trajectory, I expect total assets to be lower again at month end, continuing the established runoff pace.
Would change its mind if: Fed_assets rising above the 13w average for multiple consecutive weeks, or statement language pausing/ending balance-sheet runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

  labor_tightness lacks balance_sheet — Pace/schedule of Treasury runoff would clarify supply pressure on long-end yields.
  labor_tightness lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightening/easing would help judge risk of flight-to-quality reversal.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel this month is largely a confirmation of last meeting's picture, with one notable escalation: financial conditions (proxied here by NFCI under the "curve_slope" analyst label, which I read as the financial_conditions seat given its content) has flipped from my prior flat read to a clear, accelerating tightening trend — 7 of 8 recent prints higher, at a 52w high, momentum building across both 4w and 13w windows. That is a genuine gap-closer versus last time and pushes toward higher yields via tighter financial conditions, not looser. Meanwhile the breakeven-based inflation-expectations read continues its clean acceleration lower (12m low, accelerating 21d pace), a persistent disinflationary pull on nominal yields. The CPI/labor-market inflation and labor_tightness readings are little-changed: labor market still tight (unemployment at 12m low, no loosening), CPI flat-ish but core PCE reaccelerating — a modestly upward inflation risk that is stale/mixed rather than decisive. Term premium via balance-sheet runoff continues its uninterrupted monotonic decline, a clean tailwind for higher long-end yields through reduced Fed absorption of supply. The curve/balance_sheet-inversion analyst confirms no steepening break, curve staying deeply inverted, consistent with front-end pressure from continued hikes.

Net: the composite has shifted mildly hawkish since last month — financial conditions tightening (up-yields), continued QT (up-yields), core inflation reacceleration risk (up-yields modestly) — partially offset by falling breakevens (down-yields) and no clear labor deterioration. This tilts the level view slightly more toward "up" than my prior near-flat 0.18 conviction long, warranting a small increase in conviction and size, but not a large repositioning given persistent disagreement (breakevens vs. NFCI/QT) and inflation being genuinely split.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has re-inverted after a brief bounce: from -0.28 up to -0.25 (least inverted point), then rolling back down to -0.41 at the latest print, matching levels seen 10 observations ago. My prior "flat" call was too sanguine given the last two prints show renewed flattening (-0.39, -0.41), not stabilization. slope_change_21d is roughly flat (-0.01pp) but slope_change_63d is -0.11pp, confirming a medium-term flattening trend reasserting itself. The spread sits at just 0.06 of its 12-month range (near the 12-month low of -0.51), a deeply inverted position with essentially no cushion. The Fed's hiking language intensified (funds rate raised further to 3-3.25% with "ongoing increases" anticipated), which historically pressures the front end (2Y) more than the long end, mechanically compressing or re-inverting the curve. There's no sign of a durable reversal — the brief steepening move stalled and reversed. Given the falsifier I set previously (sustained break above -0.15 with persistent positive 21d change) has clearly not been met — the opposite occurred — I'm shifting toward a mild-to-moderate down (further flattening/re-inversion) call, though the 21d change being near-zero tempers conviction that this continues aggressively next month.

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated but momentum is now stalling. NFCI rose steadily from -0.24 to -0.10 over roughly 10 weeks (a clear tightening trend, still visible in the 13-week change of +0.09), but the last three prints are -0.10, -0.10, -0.11 — essentially flat with a tiny tick down. The 4-week change has collapsed to +0.01, confirming the trend has lost steam right at the top of its 52-week range (range_position 0.98, at the 52w high of -0.10). This is a classic deceleration/plateau pattern after a sustained easing-conditions unwind. The index remains below zero (distance_from_zero -0.11), so conditions are still slightly looser than historical average even after the tightening move. With momentum flattening and the latest tick actually negative, I don't see strong evidence for continued tightening into next month; more likely a pause or mild give-back near this multi-period high. I'm downgrading conviction on "up" substantially and leaning toward flat, with a slight tilt that a reversal (easing) is at least as plausible as further tightening given the plateau at the range ceiling.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above -0.08 with 4-week change reaccelerating past +0.03 would revive the tightening call; a drop back below -0.15 confirms reversal to easing.

The series shows a choppy but net-positive path: 21d change +0.33pp, though 63d is flat (+0.01pp) and 252d is still negative (-0.13pp), indicating the longer-term decline has stalled and reversed in the near term rather than continuing. The last two readings (2.51, 2.52) sit at the high end of recent oscillation, matching the prior peak of 2.59 seen a few observations back, suggesting expectations have stabilized in a 2.44-2.59 range with an upward bias in the most recent month. Range position (0.43) is mid-range historically, and distance from the 2% target (+0.52pp) remains elevated but not extreme. Given the clear reversal of my prior down call and the recent 21d momentum, I now lean toward continued firmness or modest further increase over the next month, though the flat 63d trend tempers conviction. This is a lean toward up, not a strong conviction call given the choppiness.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call is roughly vindicated: headline y/y fell from 8.19 to 7.76 (-0.43pp), a meaningful decline, while the mom_gap condition I set (headline MoM below 0.2%, core PCE under 5.2%) was NOT met — outgoing MoM was 0.56% and core PCE actually ticked up to 5.61 from 5.36, its highest in the sample. So the disinflation case did not clearly restore, but headline still moved down more than I expected given a sticky core.
  
  Looking at momentum: headline_3m_annualized (4.26%) is now running below the 12m trailing y/y (7.76%), consistent with further y/y deceleration mechanically as high base months roll off. But the latest MoM print (0.56%) reaccelerated from the two prior very-low prints (0.06, 0.42), and core PCE y/y is rising again, its third straight increase from 5.11 to 5.36 to 5.61 — this is the most hawkish core signal in the whole series and argues persistence, not disintegration, of underlying price pressure. Headline's y/y decline is being driven by base effects and volatile components rather than genuine disinflation, since core is diverging upward. Given the tension — headline trending down, core accelerating — I lean toward the base-effect arithmetic continuing to pull headline y/y down modestly next print, but with reduced conviction given core's inflection upward signals underlying stickiness that could stall further headline declines.
Would change its mind if: A rise in headline MoM above 0.8% or core PCE y/y continuing above 5.6% would signal renewed broad-based acceleration, invalidating the down call.

arbitrated.notes:
This panel's naming is scrambled versus my prior seat map, so I map by content: "term_premium" here is really reporting Fed balance-sheet/QT (assets falling steadily, unchanged runoff language) — that is my balance_sheet driver, still clearly down (shrinking), reaffirming upward pressure on term premium/long yields with high conviction (0.72). "balance_sheet" here is actually reporting the 2s10s curve — re-inverting, which is a curve-shape read, not a level read; I set it aside per my mandate against curve trades. "financial_conditions" here reports breakeven inflation (a level under the inflation seat, though nominally labeled financial_conditions) reversing up through my own falsifier (2.30) to 2.51-2.52 — that directly triggers the reversal condition I set last meeting and pushes toward firmer, not softer, yields. "curve_slope" reports NFCI (my actual financial_conditions driver) plateauing at a tightened level, no reversal below -0.18 — so financial conditions stay net tight-ish but momentum has stalled, a modest positive-to-neutral tilt, weaker than before. "inflation_expectations" and "inflation" both cover CPI/labor-adjacent readings with mixed signals: headyy down on base effects but core PCE accelerating three straight months — sticky, not collapsing. "labor_tightness" is oddly reporting DGS10 level/momentum, which I read as a term-premium corroboration (elevated, near range highs) rather than genuine labor data — a gap flagged by the panel itself, so I discount its labeled direction and treat it as supporting term-premium-up.

Net: my two falsifier conditions (breakeven >2.30 sustained, NFCI reversal) split — breakevens breached my trigger toward "up," NFCI did not reverse down, it just paused. QT continues unabated (strongest, freshest read). This is not a reversal of my prior modestly-up call; it is a reaffirmation, arguably strengthened by the breakeven move, tempered by NFCI's plateau and core-CPI stickiness offsetting headline softening. I hold the position, sized similarly, without adding conviction given the cross-panel confusion and staleness in two reads (16-23 days old).

Policy language reiterating that cumulative tightening effects operate with lags suggests policymakers are aware effective conditions may lag behind stated tightening, consistent with financial-market-measured conditions easing even as policy rates hold restrictive language. The headline y/y series has now declined for two straight months and sits at its 12-month low, with headline_yoy_change_3m at -1.10pp confirming clear deceleration momentum. However, the 3-month annualized headline pace (5.09%) and especially core (5.61%) remain well above the 2% target and above the recent MoM trend, meaning much of the y/y improvement is base-effect driven from last year's high prints rolling off, not necessarily current-month softness — MoM was still 0.26%, positive and non-trivial. Core PCE y/y has been essentially flat/choppy around 5.1-5.6% for many months with no clear trend, indicating underlying persistence hasn't broken down even as headline benefits from energy/food normalization. Given continued favorable base effects likely still in play for the next print, I lean toward another modest down/flat move in headline y/y, but with reduced conviction since core stickiness limits how much further deceleration is assured.

The 12-month trend is still one of a modest decline (-0.50pp), and the level remains 0.40pp below the ~4% natural-rate estimate, indicating a labor market that is tight-to-normalizing but not currently loosening. The Sahm-rule gap is trivial (+0.07pp, far below the ~0.5pp threshold that signals recession-onset loosening), and the range position (0.20) shows the rate sits near the low end of its 12-month range rather than breaking out toward the high end. Policy language reinforces this: job gains "robust," unemployment "remained low," with no signal of concern about labor-market deterioration. Absent any acceleration in the print-to-print series, there is no basis to expect a directional move away from the current 3.60% level next month. I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than last time given three consecutive identical prints reinforce genuine stability rather than noise.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening.

arbitrated.notes: Relative to last meeting, the picture has shifted modestly but not dramatically, and mostly in a direction that argues for trimming rather than reversing my mild long-duration lean. Term premium/balance-sheet: QT continues mechanically with no pause language — assets still falling, confirming the "down" (balance-sheet shrinking) call with high conviction (0.75 from the term_premium analyst, corroborated by the balance_sheet curve analyst's flattening read). This is a genuine, non-stale driver and it still argues for upward pressure on yields via reduced Fed demand/duration absorption, even though the curve-flattening itself is a shape signal I don't take a view on. Financial conditions are the key mover this month: NFCI has broken back below the analyst's own reversal threshold and is easing for six straight weeks — a fresher, higher-conviction read (0.55) than the flat/uncertain breakeven-based financial_conditions call (0.25). Easing financial conditions is disinflationary-adjacent and typically associates with lower term premium/yields, partially offsetting the QT-driven upward pull. Inflation: two independent reads (core CPI/PCE flat, headline decelerating on base effects) both point flat-to-down, with core stickiness capping how bearish-for-yields the disinflation signal can be. Labor tightness is genuinely flat (unemployment pinned at 3.60 for three prints) — no signal either way, and its own report leans on DGS10 itself as a proxy, which is circular and should be discounted for a level view. Net: the balance-sheet/QT upward pressure is intact and dominant, but is now more contested by easing financial conditions and disinflation than last month. I trim size rather than flip, keeping a small net long-yield tilt.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel this meeting is mostly a confirmation and modest deepening of last meeting's read, not a reversal. Term premium (balance_sheet driver, i.e. Fed balance sheet) confirms continued QT runoff at a steady ~$18-19B/week pace with no sign of pause — this is unambiguous and fresh, pushing yields up via reduced duration absorption (own-measurement direction: down/-0.75). The curve_slope analyst (which I read as the financial-conditions-proxy here, NFCI) shows a clean, accelerating easing trend, now confirmed fresh with no reversal — NFCI easing is a yields-up force via looser financial conditions. Actual "financial_conditions" driver (breakevens) flipped from up to down on two consecutive prints, a genuine turn from last meeting — this pulls modestly against yields, consistent with inflation expectations analyst confirming continued headline disinflation, though core PCE deceleration is slowing. Inflation driver (labor market proxy, unemployment) stays flat at cycle lows — tight labor market, a hawkish-consistent signal, unchanged from last time. Labor_tightness (10y level analysis) shows deceleration/stalling in the multi-quarter uptrend but no reversal — genuinely mixed, low conviction.

Netting these: balance sheet runoff (up-yield pressure) and financial-conditions easing (up-yield pressure via looser conditions typically preceding higher risk appetite/yields) are the freshest, most confident reads and both point the same direction as before. Breakevens softening and inflation continuing to decelerate are modest downward pulls but decelerating in their own right (momentum softening). Labor tightness offers no directional edge. This is essentially the same picture as last meeting with slightly higher conviction on the up-pressure side (QT and financial conditions easing both very fresh, high conviction) and a slightly softer inflation pull-down. I see no named gap or fresh contradiction large enough to flip the level call — if anything the evidence modestly reinforces a continued mild upward-to-flat yield bias. I hold the existing small long-yield tilt, marginally sized down given the softening breakeven/inflation crosscurrent, rather than reversing.

discounted.why: Own signal conflicted (mid-term uptrend vs recent stalling); no clean directional read to act on.

arbitrated.falsifier: A clean NFCI reversal back above -0.24 with positive momentum, or explicit Fed QT-pause language, would flip this toward flat-to-down yields; a core PCE reacceleration above 5.2% would reinforce up.

Breakeven/inflation softening could accelerate, pulling nominal yields down against the balance-sheet/FC thesis.
Panel disagreement on labor_tightness and term_premium level trend leaves the level call thinner than headline conviction suggests.

trade.rationale: Hold small long-yield tilt: fresh QT and financial-conditions-easing signals reinforce mild upward pressure, offset by softening inflation/breakeven pulls; size unchanged from last meeting given no clean reversal.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment flat at cycle lows, no new directional signal from labor driver this meeting.

The 4-week change is now only -0.01 (essentially flat) versus the steady declines seen over the 13-week window (-0.12), indicating the easing trend has lost momentum and may be inflecting. My stated falsifier condition (rebound with 4-week change turning positive) is partially met: the index rebounded off its low, though the 4-week change hasn't yet gone positive. Still, the level remains well below zero (-0.29) and below the 13-week average (-0.25), so conditions are still looser than historical average — this isn't a regime shift, just a stall/possible turn. The policy language shift from "pace" to "extent" of future increases doesn't change my read of financial conditions data itself. Given the flattening momentum and the uptick in the latest print, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than continued easing. Conviction is modest given only one data point of reversal.
Would change its mind if: A further move back above -0.24 with 4-week change clearly positive would confirm tightening; a drop back toward -0.31 or below would restore the easing call.

The policy-language edit (dropping the public-health clause) reflects one less disinflationary/uncertainty consideration in the Committee's framework, consistent with a modest firming bias rather than a dovish pivot. Momentum signals are mixed but net still favor deceleration: headline_3m_annualized (3.61%) sits well below the latest y/y print (5.96%), implying further downward pull if recent monthly prints persist. Core PCE continues to grind lower (4.94%, a fresh 13-month low) though it remains far above target (+2.94pp) and its pace of decline has slowed (core_yoy_change_3m only -0.52pp vs -1.17pp for headline), so I weight headline's momentum as the more informative signal for the next print while noting core's persistence argues against a sharp deceleration. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is negative, and the 3-month average MoM (0.30%) is modest, consistent with continued but decelerating disinflation. Policy language softened materially ("eased somewhat but remains elevated"), corroborating the data-driven easing narrative. I expect another modest decline in headline CPI y/y at the next release, though the pace of improvement is now slowing versus prior months, so conviction is moderate rather than high.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print above 0.5-0.6% or core PCE y/y ticking back up above 5.0% would signal stalling disinflation and invalidate this call.

The 3m and 6m changes are flat at +0.00pp, and the 12m change is actually -0.30pp, indicating no loosening trend at all — if anything a slight net tightening over the year. The Sahm-rule-style gap is a negligible +0.03pp, far below any threshold that would signal recessionary labor-market deterioration. My previous flat call was correct: the print came in at 3.6%, within the established range and not above my 3.70% threshold for confirming loosening. The level itself, at 3.6%, sits meaningfully below the ~4% natural rate estimate (distance -0.40pp), consistent with a still-tight labor market. Policy language continues to describe job gains as robust and unemployment as low, with no shift in tone on labor conditions specifically (the only change was dropping a public-health reference, not a labor-market characterization). With no directional momentum in either the 3m or 6m windows and the print bouncing within a narrow 0.2pp band, there is no basis to call a turn in either direction for the next report.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening beyond the recent range.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel here is confusingly labeled (two "financial_conditions"-flavored reports and two "inflation"-flavored reports, plus a "curve_slope" and "labor_tightness" whose actual content is a 10y-yield read, not jobs data). I'll map to substance rather than label. Balance-sheet (assets) confirms sustained QT with no deceleration — a clean, non-stale signal that keeps pushing yields up via reduced duration absorption/supply pressure. Term premium's own driver (labeled "term_premium" here) is actually reporting fed assets again with high conviction (-0.78) — consistent with balance_sheet, so I treat these as one reinforcing QT read, not two independent votes. On financial conditions, the two reports conflict: one (NFCI-based, "curve_slope" mislabeled) says conditions have stalled/ticked tighter after a long easing run; the other (breakeven-based, "financial_conditions" label) says breakevens made a new high, arguing for further modest tightening in inflation compensation. Both lean the same direction now — tighter/less accommodative than prior read — a reversal from my last meeting where I had financial_conditions at -0.45 (easing). This is a genuine gap-closing shift: the NFCI easing trend that anchored my prior "down" call has stalled and breakevens broke to a new high, which was explicitly one of my named flip conditions. Inflation is genuinely mixed: headline CPI still decelerating (support for lower yields) but breakevens rising (support for higher). Labor tightness (unemployment-rate report) stays flat/tight, no loosening — mildly supportive of higher yields via labor market resilience. The 10y-yield-level report shows yields creeping up near range highs, corroborating.

Net: the balance sheet impulse remains firmly toward higher yields (QT persisting, no pause signal — my named falsifier condition), and the financial-conditions easing that was propping up my "down" lean has stalled/reversed per breakevens making new highs and NFCI ticking up. This tips my prior modest long-yield-friendly tilt back toward neutral-to-slightly-up. I'm reversing my small long-duration bias to a small short-duration (higher-yield) bias, modest size given genuine disagreement across financial-conditions reports.

arbitrated.notes:
Note the naming confusion in this panel: "labor_tightness" is actually reporting on DGS10 level/momentum (not a labor market series), while "inflation" is actually reporting on the unemployment rate (labor tightness), and "inflation_expectations"/"financial_conditions" cover CPI and breakevens respectively, with a separate "curve_slope" and "balance_sheet" desk. I'm reading through to the underlying substance rather than the labels.

Substance: (1) balance_sheet/assets — Fed assets have fallen for four straight prints, 4w change clearly negative, QT continuing on schedule. Clear, fresh, high-conviction decline in balance sheet size — mechanically upward pressure on term premium/long-end yields via supply, but the driver's own measurement (assets) is falling. (2) financial_conditions proxy (breakevens) — grinding lower, accelerating decline, near 12-month lows; disinflationary signal, pulls yields down via lower inflation compensation. (3) True inflation (CPI/PCE) — flat call, deceleration has stalled, core sticky; no fresh downward inflation impulse, if anything a firming risk. (4) True labor tightness (unemployment) — at 12-month lows, tight labor market persists, mild upward wage/inflation pressure, supports yields not falling. (5) NFCI/curve_slope — conditions easing marginally after a tightening run; a modest, low-conviction easing in financial conditions, mildly yield-supportive on risk appetite grounds but not decisive. (6) Long-end level itself (mislabeled labor_tightness) shows choppy mild softening, unconfirmed.

Net: last meeting's clear disinflation impulse (falling breakevens) is confirmed and even accelerating, which argues for lower yields. But this is now offset by a labor market at multi-year tightness lows and CPI momentum stalling rather than continuing to decelerate — both slightly hawkish relative to last month. QT continuing feeds modest upward term-premium pressure. This is a more balanced, higher-disagreement picture than last meeting. I trim my tiny long-yield lean toward flat/near-zero rather than reverse it — no single driver has turned decisively enough to flip direction, but the case for staying long-yield has weakened as breakevens' decline no longer stands unopposed.

discounted.why: NFCI move is only two prints, modest and still near top of range — too thin to drive level view.

arbitrated.falsifier: A confirmed breakeven rebound above ~2.27-2.30 alongside a jobs print above 3.60% unemployment would flip this toward a clear down-yield lean; conversely a break below 3.30% unemployment plus resumed breakeven decline would support staying long-yield.

trade.rationale: Trim prior small long-yield tilt toward near-flat: disinflation signal from breakevens is offset by tight labor market and stalling CPI momentum, leaving no strong directional edge.

why.financial_conditions: Breakevens grinding to 12m lows, accelerating decline, no rebound signal — inflation compensation component clearly falling.

why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at 12m low, zero range position — labor market own-measurement (unemployment rate) still falling, i.e., tighter.
why.term_premium: NFCI easing marginally the last two prints after a tightening run, but still near top of 52w range — weak, low-conviction reversal.

With m/m running near 0.22-0.24% and less favorable base comparisons ahead, I expect headline y/y to be roughly stable to modestly higher, not a continued sharp decline. Core's stickiness reinforces that underlying inflation isn't decelerating much further, so I lean flat-to-up on headline for the next print.

arbitrated.notes:
Note the naming confusion in this panel: what's labeled "term_premium" is actually reporting Fed balance-sheet total assets (continued QT runoff, down 0.72 conviction), and what's labeled "balance_sheet" is actually reporting a curve/spread series (down 0.55, likely 2s10s-style flattening). Meanwhile "labor_tightness" is reporting the 10yr yield level/momentum (up 0.45), and "inflation" is reporting unemployment rate (flat, 0.50, at top of range). I'm reading through the mislabeling to the actual substance: balance sheet is unambiguously still shrinking with no deceleration — a persistent QT-driven upward pressure on term premium and long-end yields. That call is the freshest and highest-conviction read on the panel and it reinforces my prior thesis rather than reversing it. Financial conditions (NFCI proper) show continued but decelerating easing — modestly yield-negative but losing momentum, consistent with last meeting. Breakevens and headline/core inflation both firmed to "flat" from "down" — the disinflation narrative has stalled, removing one of the supports for lower yields I had leaned on last time. Labor market (unemployment) is flat at a tight level, no clear loosening. The yield-momentum series embedded in "labor_tightness" (10yr drifting up, near range highs) corroborates that the aggregate pressure is tilting toward higher, not lower, yields — even though this isn't a rate-level driver per se, it's directionally consistent with QT persistence and stalled disinflation.

Netting this out: last meeting I was leaning down-yield on financial conditions and term premium easing; this meeting, financial conditions eased further but decelerated, breakevens/inflation flattened out (removing the down-yield inflation-expectations support), and balance-sheet runoff continued unabated (up-yield pressure, unchanged in direction, higher conviction). The mix now nets closer to neutral-to-mildly-up on yields rather than down. I'm nudging my carried position slightly toward a modest up-yield lean rather than the near-flat +0.02/+0.02 I held, given QT persistence dominates and the inflation-expectations tailwind that previously supported "down" has faded.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel here is confusingly labeled versus my own driver taxonomy, so I'm mapping carefully: the "balance_sheet" analyst is actually discussing 2s10s slope (curve, not my seat); "curve_slope" is actually discussing NFCI (financial conditions); "financial_conditions" analyst is actually discussing breakevens (inflation expectations, not my seat per instructions); "inflation_expectations" is CPI-yoy-like data; "inflation" analyst is discussing unemployment rate (labor market); "labor_tightness" analyst is discussing the 10Y level/term premium; and "term_premium" analyst is discussing Fed balance sheet (assets). I read through the mislabeling to the actual content, since that's what matters for the level call.

Net changes since last meeting: actual balance-sheet runoff (labeled "term_premium") continues monotonically and forcefully — a clear, high-conviction, un-stale confirmation of continued QT, which is yield-supportive (higher) on the long end via reduced official-sector demand. Actual term premium proxy (labeled "labor_tightness", the 10Y level/slope momentum) continues its uptrend, reinforcing higher long-end pressure. Financial conditions (labeled "curve_slope", the NFCI data) continue to ease/loosen, which is mildly yield-supportive as looser conditions reduce downside growth risk. Inflation expectations (labeled "financial_conditions", breakevens) rose and have stabilized at a higher plateau — a genuine regime shift up, modestly firming the inflation/nominal-yield case, though momentum has stalled. Core CPI-style inflation (labeled "inflation_expectations") ticked up on a base-effect story, adding a modest upside inflation risk. Labor market tightness (labeled "inflation", the unemployment-rate view) is flat/rangebound, no directional info. The curve-shape analyst (labeled "balance_sheet") shows tentative steepening stabilization but is explicitly low-conviction and not confirmed across horizons — I set this aside as it's a curve call, not a level call, and stale/inconclusive besides.

Combining: balance-sheet runoff continuing forcefully, term-premium-proxy uptrend intact, financial conditions loosening, and breakevens holding a higher plateau all point the same direction — modestly higher yields, similar in shape to my prior call but reinforced by the fact that my own stated falsifier condition (breakevens above ~2.24-2.27 sustained) has now clearly triggered without any QT pause. That is directly on my falsifier language and should firm, not merely maintain, my prior lean. I am nudging conviction up slightly from last meeting given this confirmation, while keeping the position modest since curve/level cross-currents (deep 2s10s inversion, some stalling in breakeven momentum) argue against a large duration bet.

I'll raise conviction modestly given confirmed reacceleration, though core's continued cooling tempers how far I lean.

The unrate_range_position sits at 1.00 (top of the 12-month range), and the sahm_gap (0.20pp) has widened. My prior flat call was wrong in direction terms — this is no longer sideways chop, it's a break to a new high. One month alone doesn't confirm a Sahm-rule trigger (which needs a 0.50pp rise from the 12-month low sustained), but the change_3m and change_12m both ticking to +0.10pp while the single-month move was +0.20pp suggests acceleration. I'm calling this a loosening labor market, not yet severe, but a genuine shift, not a level call.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 3.50% or below next month would suggest this was noise; a further rise toward 3.80%+ would confirm a genuine loosening trend.

arbitrated.notes:
Something odd here: the panel names appear scrambled relative to their content. There's "curve_slope" and "inflation_expectations" and a duplicate "inflation" entry that are not in my mandate list, alongside proper "balance_sheet," "financial_conditions," "labor_tightness," "term_premium" reports. I'll map content to my five sanctioned drivers by what each report actually measures: balance_sheet (Fed assets, -0.76, flat/choppy), term_premium (this meeting's report actually measures Fed total assets shrinking — same series as balance_sheet's own report, confusingly labeled "down, 0.78" but describing QT continuing, i.e. balance-sheet runoff, not term premium proper) — I'll treat the assets-runoff report as the authoritative balance_sheet read since it's fresher and more decisive than the "balance_sheet" analyst's own flat call, given the explicit gap dependency and the unbroken 13-week decline. financial_conditions genuinely reports NFCI-linked easing (the "curve_slope" entry) plus breakevens falling (the "financial_conditions" entry) — both point the same direction: financial conditions easing. Inflation: headline CPI reaccelerating (3.72% yoy, hot m/m) is the clear, fresh, decisive signal — core cooling is a secondary nuance. Labor: the "inflation" entry actually describes unemployment rate rising to a 12-month high — that's my labor_tightness read (loosening labor market). The genuine "labor_tightness" report (about DGS10 momentum) isn't really about labor tightness at all and gives no labor information — I discount it as mislabeled/uninformative for my mandate.

Synthesis for yield level: balance-sheet runoff continuing unabated is a base-case upward pressure on term premium/yields (supply-driven). But financial conditions are easing fast (NFCI at 52-week loose extreme, breakevens falling), which is disinflationary and yield-suppressing. Headline CPI reaccelerating argues for higher yields near-term, but labor market loosening (unemployment at 12-month high, Sahm gap widening) argues for lower yields via growth/Fed-cut expectations. This is a much more balanced, cross-cutting picture than my prior meeting: QT still pushes up, but easing financial conditions and rising unemployment now countervail the inflation-reacceleration and QT theses more directly than before. Net, I shade my long-standing modest up-yield lean down in size, keeping direction but trimming conviction, since the panel is now genuinely split on the rate axis rather than uniformly hawkish.
