The 21d (+0.47pp) and 63d (+0.78pp) changes show sustained upward momentum, not the stalling I had guessed. The last two prints (4.59, 4.59) suggest some flattening in the very latest days, but the broader trend over the past one-three months is clearly higher yields. The 30s10s slope is modestly positive (+0.14pp), consistent with mild curve steepening at the long end rather than a bull-flattening driven by term-premium compression — this supports a rising-premium narrative rather than a growth-scare/flight-to-quality one. Continued Fed balance-sheet runoff (per the unchanged policy language) keeps a structural supply/duration overhang on the long end, consistent with upward pressure on term premium. Given the level is at a 12-month high, momentum is positive across multiple windows, and the slope confirms a genuine long-end (not just curve-shape) move, I now see the term premium as rising, though the very last two flat prints warrant some caution about calling this an unambiguous acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below ~4.45 with negative 21d momentum, or a sharp flattening/inversion in 30s10s, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

There is no sign of a flattening trigger (no flat/rising weekly print, no reversal above 8.3T, no policy shift). Given the consistency of the trend across 4w, 13w, and 52w windows, and unchanged reinvestment language, I maintain a high-conviction down call for month-end, expecting total assets to continue falling at a similar or slightly faster pace.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print flat or rising, sustained reversal above ~8.3T, or policy language announcing a pause/taper/end of runoff.

arbitrated.notes:
There's a naming confusion in this panel: the analyst labeled "labor_tightness" is actually reporting on DGS10 level/term-premium-like dynamics (10y yield, 30s10s slope), while the analyst labeled "term_premium" is actually reporting balance-sheet (Fed assets) data, and "balance_sheet" is reporting curve slope (2s10s). I'm reading through to what each is actually measuring rather than the label. Net: (1) Fed balance sheet continues to shrink at an unabated or slightly accelerating pace, unambiguous and high-conviction — mechanically supportive of higher long-end yields via reduced duration absorption. (2) The 10y yield itself has climbed to a 12-month high with confirmed momentum and a steepening 30s10s, i.e., term premium is rising — genuine long-end pressure, not just curve mechanics. (3) 2s10s has been steadily steepening/de-inverting, consistent with the market pricing a Fed hold and less front-end anchoring, another modest yield-supportive signal for the belly/long end. (4) Financial conditions (NFCI) troughed and has ticked back up slightly — easing has stalled, arguing against additional downward yield pressure from loose conditions, though the magnitude is tiny and this is a low-conviction flat call. (5) Inflation is genuinely mixed: headline CPI stabilizing/plateauing with a slight upward lean given 3m annualized pace, but core PCE decelerating steadily — a wash, modest upside lean only. (6) Labor market (unemployment) plateaued at a 12-month high, deceleration in loosening pace — mixed, slight tightening-adjacent read but not a strong signal either way.

Overall the fresh, high-conviction reads (balance sheet runoff continuing, 10y momentum/term premium rising, curve de-inverting) all point the same direction: modest upward pressure on nominal yield levels. This confirms and modestly reinforces my prior long-duration-averse (short/underweight duration, i.e., long yield) stance rather than reversing it. Financial conditions stabilizing (no longer easing) removes one prior downward force. I'll size up slightly given the convergence of three independent, freshly-formed signals rather than the single stale balance-sheet read I leaned on last time.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The most recent print (-0.19) sits within a tight recent band (-0.13 to -0.19 over the last several observations) after a substantial steepening move over the past several months (slope_change_63d +0.68pp, slope_change_252d +0.15pp). My prior "up" call was directionally correct over the longer window — the curve has steepened materially from 12m lows (-1.08pp) and now sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.94, only 0.13pp below the 12m high). However, the most recent few observations show stalling/mild reversal: after reaching -0.13, the spread eased back to -0.15/-0.15/-0.19, and slope_change_21d is still positive (+0.25) but decelerating versus the 63d pace, suggesting the steepening impulse is losing momentum near range highs. The Fed statement shift (holding rates, "solid pace" language replacing "moderate pace" and dropping a hike) suggests less near-term tightening bias, which could still support modest further steepening, but with the spread already near 12-month highs and recent prints ticking back down, the risk/reward for further steepening is limited near-term. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up, but with much lower conviction than before given the plateau/reversal at the range top.
Would change its mind if: A clean break below -0.25 sustained for multiple sessions with slope_change_21d turning negative would confirm renewed flattening rather than a pause.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_above_12m_low

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series bottomed at -0.34 mid-series and has since risen steadily to -0.28, the least-easy reading in this window. The 4-week change is now clearly positive (+0.04), reversing the prior easing trend, even though the 13-week change is still slightly negative (-0.02) because it spans the earlier decline. Momentum has clearly turned: the last six observations show a consistent, uninterrupted climb from -0.34 to -0.28, indicating tightening conditions over the past month. The index remains well below the zero average and in the loose portion of its 52-week range (range_position 0.24), so conditions are still historically accommodative in absolute terms, but the direction of travel is now unambiguously toward tightening. My prior flat call was wrong in retrospect — the 4-week momentum had already begun to build, and it has now sustained for six straight readings, satisfying my own falsifier condition (sustained positive 4-week change with a move toward -0.28). Given this consistent short-term reversal, I expect the index to continue drifting modestly higher (tighter) into month end, absent any new easing shock, though the move so far is gradual rather than sharp.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back toward -0.34 or a flattening of the 4-week change near zero would indicate the tightening move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

Call: flat, conviction 0.25

  The last print (2.42) sits right in the middle of recent chop, having oscillated 2.44→2.49→2.47→2.41→2.40→2.43→2.42→2.42→2.44→2.42. My prior "flat" call holds up reasonably well: the series has not sustained a break above 2.45 or below 2.30, staying inside a narrow band. The 21d change (+0.07) and 63d change (+0.05) are modestly positive, but the 252d change is slightly negative (-0.03), and the last three prints (2.42, 2.44, 2.42) show no clear trend — essentially flat-lining after the mid-series dip and partial recovery. Range position at 0.74 shows breakevens are in the upper part of their 12-month range, still comfortably above the 12m low (2.10) and 42bp above the 2% target, indicating expectations remain anchored somewhat above target but not accelerating. There's no fresh directional impulse in the last few observations — the modest uptrend over 21-63 days has stalled at the same level for three consecutive prints. Absent a break of the 2.45 ceiling or 2.40 floor with conviction, I see this as continued range-bound behavior rather than a new trend, supporting a flat call again, though the slightly positive medium-term drift keeps me from full conviction on flat.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.30) was wrong: headline yoy fell from 3.69 to 3.25, and the m/m print (0.14) came in below my own 0.15% falsifier threshold, confirming disinflation rather than the reacceleration I was leaning toward. Looking at the full picture now: headline yoy has round-tripped from a mid-series bump (3.29→3.72→3.69) back down to 3.25, essentially near the 12-month low of 3.07. Core PCE continues a steady, uninterrupted descent (3.83→3.69), now 1.69pp above target, with 3m annualized core running at just 2.26% — close to target-consistent pace. Headline 3m annualized (4.12%) is noisier and still elevated, but the latest single-month print was soft (0.14% m/m, below the 3m average of 0.34%). The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is small and positive, but the outright yoy drop of 0.44pp in one month is the dominant signal. Weighting core for persistence (given headline's energy/food noise) and noting the actual outcome versus my prior expectation, the momentum is disinflationary, not stalling. I don't see strong evidence for a rebound next month — no base effects or acceleration signals here — so I expect headline yoy to be flat-to-slightly-down, not up.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or yoy rising back above 3.6% would signal renewed acceleration rather than continued disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call was flat with modest conviction, and it was wrong: the newest print jumped to 3.90% from 3.70%, exactly the kind of push above 3.80% I flagged as the trigger for renewed loosening. The unemployment rate has now risen 0.40pp over 3 months and 0.50pp over 6 months, sitting at the 12-month high and a full 0.50pp above the 12-month low (3.40%). The Sahm-style gap (0.37pp) is approaching levels historically associated with turning points. This is not a single noisy tick — the last three readings (3.70, 3.70, 3.90) show consecutive increases after a period of flat oscillation between 3.40-3.60%, suggesting a genuine trend shift rather than measurement noise. While the level itself (3.90%) is still only slightly above a 4% natural rate estimate, the rate-of-change is the more important signal here per the Sahm-rule spirit, and it argues for continued loosening rather than stabilization. Policy language also shifted from "robust" to "slowed" job gains, consistent with cooling. I'm revising toward a directional call: the unemployment rate is more likely to tick up or hold at this elevated level than to reverse sharply back down next month, given the momentum evident in the trailing three prints.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 3.60-3.70% would confirm the 3.90 print was noise; another rise to 4.0%+ would confirm accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high

The 30s10s slope remains modestly positive (+0.16pp), showing no flattening/inversion signal that would flag a shift back toward flight-to-quality or a stalling long end. Continued balance-sheet runoff (per unchanged policy language) keeps a structural bid against duration, consistent with upward pressure on long-end premium. That said, the latest two prints (4.84, 4.88) show yields oscillating in a tight band just below the cycle high, suggesting the sharpest leg of the move may be pausing rather than accelerating further. I still lean toward modest further upside or at least persistence near cycle highs, but conviction is tempered by the flattening of very recent momentum compared to the 63d/252d trend.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below ~4.45 with negative 21d momentum, or a sharp flattening/inversion in 30s10s, would reverse this call.

arbitrated.notes: There appear to be two analysts miscoded under wrong labels this round (an "inflation" analyst reporting on unemployment rate, and a "labor_tightness" analyst reporting on DGS10 level) plus a new "curve_slope" voice — I'm reading past the label mix-up to the substance. Net effect: balance-sheet runoff (term_premium driver) continues unabated and even accelerating slightly — assets down again, no pause language, confident -0.80 call. That is the single freshest, most mechanical, least noisy driver on the desk and it argues for continued upward pressure on term premium / long-end yields via shrinking Fed footprint. Financial conditions are flat-to-tightening (NFCI momentum turned, six straight upticks) which is a modest additional lean toward higher yields at the margin (less accommodation). The balance_sheet-labeled analyst (curve slope in bank reserves/steepening terms) is now flagging exhaustion near range highs — deceleration, not reversal — so I keep it as a soft tailwind, not a fresh driver. Inflation expectations proper (CPI/PCE) rolled over hard (yoy 3.69→3.25, core decelerating) — a genuine disinflation surprise that undercuts the yields-up case somewhat, but it's offset by the unemployment-side reading showing labor loosening/Sahm-rule proximity, which is yields-ambiguous (loosening labor typically pulls yields down via growth channel, but the drop is only mild so far and could still reflect noise). On net: QT (term premium proxy) and financial-conditions tightening are the freshest, most decisive signals, both consistent with continued modest upward yield pressure; disinflation is the one clear countervailing force. This roughly reaffirms the prior long-and-short duration-up position but conviction is not rising given the split between inflation cooling and everything else supporting higher yields. I hold the existing modest steepener-tilted long-yield stance without resizing.

arbitrated.notes: This panel has some naming confusion vs. last meeting — the labels shifted (balance_sheet now reports steepening curve data, curve_slope reports NFCI, financial_conditions reports breakevens, inflation_expectations reports CPI, inflation reports unemployment, labor_tightness reports DGS10 levels). Reading through to substance rather than labels: the actual balance-sheet runoff series (the one showing weekly asset declines, "term_premium" label this round) is unambiguous — 13 straight weekly declines, no deceleration, policy language unchanged, conviction 0.80. That is the single freshest, most mechanical, least noisy driver on the panel, and it argues for continued upward pressure on yields via reduced Fed demand/duration absorption. The disinflation trend (CPI/PCE, "inflation_expectations" label) is also fairly fresh and confirms continued but decelerating disinflation (down, 0.35) — pulls yields down modestly. The unemployment/labor slack series ("inflation" label) is stale-ish (15 days) and borderline flat/mild-loosening — small downward pull, low conviction. The DGS10-momentum-based "labor_tightness" report shows the level actually rolling over recently (21d -0.51pp) even though longer trend is up — this is a market-based read, somewhat circular with the trade itself, and it flags gaps versus financial conditions and balance sheet that weren't fed to it. NFCI ("curve_slope") shows financial conditions have re-eased after a brief tightening — mild dovish/down-yield pull, moderate conviction. Net: balance-sheet runoff (fresh, high conviction, structurally upward on yields) is the dominant force, partially offset by disinflation and easier financial conditions. This is essentially the same net picture as last meeting — balance sheet dominant, inflation and financial conditions partial offsets — so I hold the existing modest long-duration-yield (short bonds) position rather than resize on a single month's wobble in the DGS10 momentum series.

The panel this meeting has shifted materially versus my prior read, and I need to update accordingly rather than just hold. Balance sheet (2s10s slope): last meeting I read this as a clear steepener (up-yield pressure); now the analyst reports the steepening impulse has faded, 63d change negative, and the last two prints are the least steep in the series — a modest down/flattening bias, much weaker signal. Financial conditions (NFCI, via curve_slope's fresher read) shows a fresh 52-week low, uninterrupted grind lower, no stalling — this is the strongest, freshest, most unambiguous signal on the panel and it points to looser financial conditions, which is disinflationary/yield-suppressing. The financial_conditions analyst itself (breakevens) has moved from down to flat — stabilizing, not a driver of further downside. Inflation: CPI/PCE data confirms continued disinflation (core PCE down to 3.11%, 3m annualized soft), a fresher and more decisive read than the labor-market-based inflation proxy which is flat/noisy. Labor tightness: unemployment ticked down to 3.70%, a genuine tightening signal, but conviction is capped by acknowledged noise and missing cross-reads (financial conditions, curve context) that could explain it as risk-off rather than labor strength. Term premium (balance sheet runoff) continues down but decelerating, with a tiny uptick — my falsifier condition (pause/taper) hasn't fired, but deceleration is evidence of a fading tailwind for the down-call, so I keep it down but softened.

Netting this out: disinflation (yields down), looser financial conditions (yields down), decelerating QT runoff (still down but weaker), tightening labor (yields up, modest), and a curve/slope call that's now ambiguous rather than clearly steepening. The composite is a modest net downward pull on yields, reinforcing rather than reversing my prior read, but the balance-sheet driver's reversal from steepening to flattening removes one of the up-yield pressures I had been counting on. I'll size down slightly and lean back toward a small net-lower yield stance rather than the marginal-up carry I've held.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel this meeting is clearly mislabeled relative to my seat conventions, so I'm mapping by content, not by header. There are effectively two inflation reads (CPI-headline reaccelerating strongly, up 0.55; and a second "inflation" read that's really the labor/unemployment series, flat-to-modestly-up on Sahm gap), a "labor_tightness" entry that is actually describing DGS10 level/momentum (flat, weak), a "term_premium" entry that is actually balance-sheet/QT runoff (down assets, clear and undecelerated, conviction 0.75), a "balance_sheet" entry that is actually curve-slope data (2s10s/10s30s, modest re-flattening lean), a "financial_conditions" entry on breakevens (flat/range-bound), and "curve_slope" on NFCI (flat, momentum stalling).

Synthesizing for the level view: the clearest, freshest, highest-conviction signal is continued balance-sheet runoff (labeled term_premium here) — QT is proceeding steadily with no pause signaled, which is a structural upward pressure on term premium/long yields. This is the one driver that both stayed on-call and strengthened in conviction from last meeting. Inflation (headline CPI) also strengthened notably — three consecutive hot m/m prints, 3m annualized at 4.75%, a genuine upside surprise versus my prior 0.45 call, even though core PCE continues to decelerate underneath. That's a genuine upgrade. Financial conditions/breakevens have gone flat and range-bound, no longer supportive of the "up" case I had before — a real downgrade from 0.35 to near flat. Labor/unemployment (Sahm gap rising but still sub-threshold) is a mild, low-conviction loosening signal, ambiguous for yields. The DGS10-momentum entry mislabeled as labor_tightness shows near-term consolidation, not a fresh selloff or rally, but a still-elevated level.

Net: continued QT runoff plus reaccelerating headline inflation now outweigh the fading financial-conditions upside case. This modestly *strengthens* my prior mild bearish-yield lean (I was carrying small negative duration) rather than reversing it. I'll size up slightly on the belly/long end given firmer QT + inflation conviction, keeping the position modest given the internal panel confusion and mixed labor signal.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel this month is confusing at the label level but resolves fairly cleanly once mapped to what each analyst is actually measuring. Note the naming mismatch: the "labor_tightness" analyst is actually reporting on DGS10 yield momentum (not labor data), while "inflation" and "inflation_expectations" both cover CPI/PCE, and "curve_slope"/"financial_conditions" both cover NFCI-type conditions. I treat these as genuine duplicate/overlapping reads and weight accordingly.

Balance-sheet: still the highest-conviction, freshest driver. QT continues unabated (-$82B/4wk, -$275B/13wk), policy language unchanged, no taper signal. This is a clear, structural, non-stale force pushing yields up via reduced duration absorption capacity — even though the driver's own "measurement" (assets) is falling, that's yield-positive.

Inflation: two reads, both converging on "flat," a real softening from my prior "up" call. CPI m/m decelerated to 0.22%, y/y ticked down from 3.49 to 3.36. My prior +0.55 conviction is no longer supported — this driver has plateaued, not accelerated. I'm cutting inflation conviction sharply toward flat.

Labor/term-premium-as-DGS10: this "labor_tightness" read is actually just describing DGS10's own upward drift — circular for a duration view, so I heavily discount it as a level input (it's the effect, not an independent cause).

Financial conditions: NFCI and financial_conditions both rangebound/flat, marginal tightening tilt but noise-level. Not a strong lever either way.

Net: balance-sheet (QT, structural, up-yield) and the resolved-flat inflation are the two genuine, live inputs. Financial conditions contribute a mild up-tilt. This is somewhat softer than last meeting's blend (inflation was overstated then), but the QT force alone still argues for holding a modest bear-steepening-adjacent duration short, not reversing it. I trim conviction slightly given inflation's climbdown.

arbitrated.notes:
There's important mislabeling in this panel to sort through first. The analyst tagged "balance_sheet" is actually reporting on the 2s10s slope (a curve-shape metric, not the Fed balance sheet), and "labor_tightness" is reporting DGS10 levels/30s10s slope (a rates/term-premium proxy, not labor data), while "inflation" is actually reporting unemployment-rate data (Sahm gap, unrate distance from natural rate) — a labor signal mislabeled as inflation. Meanwhile "term_premium" is the one giving me the actual Fed balance-sheet (total assets/QT pace) data, and "inflation_expectations" is giving me the real CPI/core-PCE trajectory. I'm reading the content, not the labels, and mapping to my seat's five canonical drivers accordingly.

On that basis: balance-sheet (true Fed assets) continues to shrink, confirmed fresh at this meeting — assets down again, QT intact just decelerating. That's a runoff-driven upward pressure on term premium/yields, though the deceleration (cap cut $60B→$25B) tempers the magnitude versus last month. Inflation (true CPI/PCE) surprised to the downside this meeting — negative m/m, decelerating core PCE — a genuine reversal of my prior "up" lean, now clearly disinflationary. The unemployment-rate proxy (mislabeled "inflation") shows unrate jumping to a 12-month high, signaling labor loosening (tighter->looser), which is disinflationary/dovish for yields too. Financial conditions (NFCI) drifted toward mild tightening, a small yield-supportive push. Curve-content analysts (2s10s, DGS10-level) I set aside as out-of-seat for a duration/level call, though their steepening bias is a directional hint I discount since it's curve-shape territory.

Net: the QT-driven upward pressure I had is now more contested — real inflation data flipped meaningfully lower, and labor is loosening, both pulling yields down, while balance-sheet still exerts modest upward pressure via reduced-but-continuing runoff. This nets out closer to flat, marginally down, versus my prior mild-down stance. I keep the position but trim conviction given the softer inflation surprise now offsetting the balance-sheet call.

arbitrated.notes: There's a naming mismatch this meeting: the driver labeled "financial_conditions" in this panel is actually reporting on breakeven/inflation-expectations series (2.24-2.31 range, "distance from 2% target" language), while "inflation_expectations" itself separately confirms CPI/PCE disinflation. Both point the same direction — inflation expectations and breakevens grinding lower — so I read these as corroborating, not conflicting, and treat the combined signal as a clear down-lean on the inflation axis. The driver actually labeled "inflation" here is really covering unemployment (Sahm-gap, unrate) — i.e., labor_tightness's usual territory — and it shows unemployment rising toward 4.20%, a loosening labor market. Meanwhile "labor_tightness" itself is reporting on DGS10 level/momentum, which is really the term_premium driver's usual territory, and it confirms term premium richening (10y down to 4.09, falling). The genuine term_premium driver (fed assets) confirms continued balance-sheet runoff, a modest upward force on yields via reduced demand absorption, but decelerating pace tempers it. Net: disinflation continuing, labor market loosening (unemployment rising = less tight, which is disinflationary/growth-negative for yields), term premium/duration premium compressing (10y richening), and balance-sheet runoff a modest offsetting upward force that's decelerating. All the "soft" forces (falling inflation, loosening labor, falling term premium) outweigh the modest QT drag. This is consistent with and reinforces my prior down-tilt call — no driver flipped against me; if anything the down case strengthened (unemployment now confirmed rising for 4 straight months, breakevens grinding to 12m-low-adjacent levels). I'll modestly deepen the existing small short-duration-yield (long price) tilt rather than resize dramatically, since the moves are gradual/decelerating, not sharp inflections.

My stated falsifier — two consecutive flat readings at 4.20% — has technically been met, which argues for tempering conviction that the rise is still accelerating. However, the broader trend remains clearly one of loosening: the rate is up 0.5pp over 12 months and 0.3pp over 3-6 months, sitting at the top of its 12-month range (range_position 1.00), a full 0.47pp above the trailing 12-month low (Sahm-gap territory), and 0.20pp above the ~4% natural-rate anchor. Given the level is now meaningfully above the recent low and off natural-rate anchor, and there's no sign of reversal back toward 4.00% or below, I lean toward continued softness but with reduced conviction versus a clean upward call, since the immediate momentum has stalled.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 4.00% or below, or a third consecutive flat print at 4.20%, would confirm stabilization rather than continued loosening.

arbitrated.notes:
Reconciling against last meeting: I had a modest duration-long tilt (yields down) built on soft inflation, loosening labor, easing financial conditions, term-premium compression from QT-driven curve dynamics, and balance-sheet drag. This meeting's panel mostly confirms the structural pieces but tempers the near-term momentum meaningfully.

Inflation: the CPI-facing analyst now calls flat (0.35 conv) after a sharp mean-reverting base-effect drop, with core PCE actually ticking up — this is a genuine softening of my prior "down" conviction, not just noise. The labor-market-facing "inflation" analyst (unemployment) still says up/loosening but flags its own falsifier (two flat 4.20% prints) has technically triggered, cutting momentum. Labor_tightness (the analyst using DGS10 as proxy, oddly) shows short-term yield reversal higher — this is really a duration-relevant read directly: 10y drifting up over last three prints, near 12-month lows, mean-reversion risk. That's a live signal pushing against my long-duration position.

Financial conditions: NFCI tightening tick is modest and wobbly (curve_slope analyst), breakevens stabilizing not falling (financial_conditions analyst) — both now "flat" rather than the "down/easing" I had, which was itself supportive of lower yields via risk-off. That support has weakened.

Term premium (balance sheet proxy here) still shows unambiguous continued QT runoff, no reversal — this is the one driver still cleanly confirming upward pressure on term premium / yields, unchanged conviction.

Balance sheet analyst (2s10s) shows curve steepening plateauing at zero — curve-shape info, not my lane, but confirms no acute flight-to-quality flattening that would argue for much lower yields.

Net: the loosening-labor/disinflation story that justified my long-duration tilt has lost momentum on several fronts simultaneously (inflation flattening, financial conditions flattening, and a direct yield-momentum signal turning higher), while QT continues unabated. This is enough convergent softening to trim, not reverse, my long-duration position.

Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.

arbitrated.notes:
Confusing labeling this round: the analyst named "term_premium" is actually reporting on Fed balance sheet mechanics (assets falling ~-878k over 52w, confirmed QT continuing at a slightly faster pace), while the analyst named "balance_sheet" is actually reading 2s10s curve slope. I'm mapping content to function: continued balance-sheet runoff (down, high conviction 0.72) still argues for QT-driven upward pressure on term premium/long-end supply — consistent with my prior call, and reaffirmed with fresh, non-stale data this meeting. Financial conditions (NFCI) show a persistent, unbroken easing trend (13 straight non-reversing prints, at 52-week low) — that's a fresher and more confident read than last meeting's flat call, and easing financial conditions historically remove some safety-bid support for duration, a mild yield-up force. Separately, financial_conditions-as-labeled (breakevens) reports flat-to-slightly-up, consistent with last time, low conviction. Inflation signals are split: inflation_expectations (CPI/PCE) show reacceleration in momentum (up, 0.45), while inflation-labeled analyst (unemployment/Sahm) reports plateau (flat). Net inflation read: modest upward drift, but not clean.

Given: continued QT (yields up pressure), easing financial conditions per NFCI (also modestly yields-up via risk-appetite/carry, though this cuts against duration demand), and firming inflation momentum — the net vector still points to modest upward pressure on yields, consistent with my prior small short-duration tilt. The curve-slope steepening (front end grinding lower on cuts) is a curve signal, not a level call, so I set it aside per mandate. No driver has flipped decisively enough to reverse the position; if anything, QT and inflation momentum reinforce it. I hold the position essentially unchanged, keeping the modest positive-yield tilt on both legs.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

Report:
  The 2s10s spread has drifted down to 0.05 from a recent range near 0.09-0.13, with slope_change_21d at -0.12pp — a clear negative move over the past month. This is exactly the falsifier condition I set previously: a drop to/below 0.05 with 21d change clearly negative. My prior "up" call was wrong; the curve has flattened, not steepened, over the last month. Longer-term context still shows steepening (slope_change_252d +0.50pp, range_position 0.73 — spread sits in upper part of 12-month range), so this isn't a collapse back toward deep inversion, but the recent trend is a pause/reversal in the steepening trend rather than continuation. The 63d change is only +0.04pp, essentially flat, confirming the steepening has stalled. The Fed's shift to smaller rate cuts (1/4 point vs prior 1/2 point) suggests a slower, more gradual easing path, which could keep front-end yields somewhat supported and cap further curve steepening near-term, consistent with the recent flattening tick. Absent a clear catalyst to resume steepening, I expect the spread to stay near current depressed levels or drift slightly lower over the next month, with genuine uncertainty given the choppy recent action (0.09→0.06→0.05).

The 4-week change (-0.03) and 13-week change (-0.09) both confirm a persistent, monotonic easing trend rather than a stall — the last observation is the lowest in the window and the series has fallen in nearly every consecutive reading with no reversal. My prior "down" call at 0.60 conviction was correct: conditions kept easing, not tightening. The range_position of 0.00 confirms we are sitting at the bottom of the observed 52-week range, and there's no sign of flattening — the 4-week change magnitude is similar to prior periods, suggesting the trend has momentum rather than exhaustion. Absent any policy language pointing to a shift, and with no measurement showing deceleration in the pace of decline, the most reasonable one-month-ahead call remains continued easing (lower NFCI). I'd flag that the pace (-0.01/week average) is modest, so this is a clear but not extreme signal — a plateau near current lows is plausible, but the data gives no actual evidence of one yet.
Would change its mind if: A reading back above -0.43 (13-week average) or a flattening of the 4-week change toward zero would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.

My prior "flat" call is now looking stale — the sequence 2.34,2.34,2.29,2.28,2.26 is a persistent, monotonic decline over the last five prints, not noise within a range. Range position at 0.59 (of the 12-month 2.02-2.43 band) is still mid-range, not at an extreme, and the level remains 0.26pp above the 2% target, so this isn't a dramatic dislocation. But the direction of travel over the most recent weeks is unambiguously down, and there's no sign of reversal in the last three points. I'll shift from flat to a modest downward lean, since the move is real but not yet large enough (still within 12-month range, not breaching 2.20) to warrant high conviction of a sustained trend break.

Report:

Momentum confirms this isn't noise: headline mom printed 0.28% (above both outgoing 0.19% and the 3m avg 0.26%), and headline 3m annualized sits at 3.18%, well above the current y/y rate — meaning the y/y figure has further upside baked in mechanically as soft months roll off. Core PCE y/y also climbed to 2.99 from 2.84, its third consecutive increase off the 2.75 low, with core 3m annualized at 3.03%, reinforcing that underlying persistence has stopped decelerating. The removal of "further" and "gained greater confidence" from the policy language (softened confidence framing) is consistent with officials recognizing this stall/reacceleration rather than continued disinflation. Both headline and core now agree on direction, which strengthens conviction versus prior instances of divergence. Level is still moderate (yoy_range_position 0.28, core still ~1pp above target) so this isn't alarming, but momentum clearly points up for the next print given the annualized run-rate gap versus y/y.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.15% or core PCE y/y reversing back under 2.85% would flip this toward flat.

Call: up, conviction 0.45

Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The last print rose to 4.20% from 4.10%, matching the cycle high and triggering the falsifier I set previously (a print above/at the prior high after two prints at 4.10%). My prior "flat" call was too sanguine given the data was already at the top of its range with a 0.43pp Sahm gap. The 12-month trend is clearly up (+0.50pp), the 3m average sits at 4.13% versus a 12m low of 3.70%, and the rate is now pinned at the top of its 13-month range (range_position = 1.00). While the 3m change is technically flat (4.10→4.10→4.20 oscillation), the level has now printed at the cycle high twice in the last four readings, and policy language has shifted to acknowledge labor conditions have "generally eased" — a more explicit softening characterization than the prior statement's narrower framing. This is consistent with a gradual loosening trend rather than a stable plateau. I do not see evidence of re-tightening (no move back toward 3.9% or below). Given the persistent upward drift, proximity to the Sahm threshold, and confirmatory policy language, I lean toward another modest increase or at least a hold at the elevated level, but conviction is moderate since the 3m change is flat and one print isn't decisive confirmation of accelerating deterioration.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 4.0% or below would signal the rise was noise rather than a genuine loosening trend.

The 21-day change is -0.10pp and the trend within the last few readings shows a clear step-down (4.41→4.27→4.30→4.25→4.18), suggesting downward momentum has picked up even though the 63-day change is still positive (+0.34pp) reflecting the earlier run-up. Range position at 0.51 is mid-range, not extreme, so this isn't a low-yield extreme, just a fresh pullback. The 30s10s slope at +0.18pp is modest, not showing aggressive steepening that would signal renewed term-premium building at the long end; 10s2s at +0.05 is flat. My prior 'flat' call was too cautious — the recent run of lower prints is a real, if not yet dramatic, down-move. Given continued balance-sheet runoff pressure as a persistent upward force, I don't expect a large decline, but the immediate trend and proximity to the 4.15 reversal marker argue for leaning down rather than flat for the coming month.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.35 with steepening 30s10s would confirm reversal upward; a break clearly below 4.15 would confirm this down call.

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct and the trend has if anything accelerated. Total assets fell every single week across the 13-week window, with no counter-week at all. The 4-week change (-108,350) is larger in magnitude than one-third of the 13-week change annualized pace would suggest, indicating the runoff pace picked up rather than decelerated — consistent with continued balance-sheet normalization at or near the announced monthly caps. The 52-week change (-891,005) confirms a sustained, large-scale multi-quarter drawdown, and assets sit ~834,426 below the 52-week high with no sign of basing. Policy language remains unchanged: the Committee continues to reduce holdings of Treasuries, agency debt, and agency MBS, with no mention of tapering, pausing, or ending runoff. Given the steady weekly declines, an accelerating 4-week pace, and stable policy language reaffirming continued reduction, the highest-probability outcome for month end is another decline in total assets, roughly in line with or slightly larger than the recent 4-week pace (~$100-110B). No evidence of a policy pivot or reserve-scarcity concern is present. I hold conviction fairly high given the unanimity of the weekly declines and unchanged guidance.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.

arbitrated.notes:
Labels here are confusing across meetings because the panel's naming has drifted: what's now called "term_premium" is actually reporting Fed balance-sheet (total assets) data, continuing straight-line runoff at an accelerating 4-week pace with no basing — this is the clearest, highest-conviction driver on the panel and it argues for continued upward pressure on term premium / long-end yields via reduced Fed demand. What's now labeled "balance_sheet" is actually reporting 2s10s curve slope, which the analyst flags as a flattening reversal — informative for curve but I don't take a level view from it directly. "financial_conditions" here is reporting breakeven inflation data turning down over the last three prints — a soft signal toward lower inflation compensation, tempering the term-premium-up read. The separate "curve_slope" driver is actually the true NFCI financial-conditions read, and it's unambiguous: a fresh 52-week low, monotonic decline, no reversal — that's a genuine, non-stale easing-in-financial-conditions signal, which is disinflationary for the level and argues for capped/lower yields on the front end especially. Inflation (unemployment-mislabeled series, actually CPI/PCE) is up convincingly — headline and core both accelerating, momentum confirmed by 3m annualized rates well above y/y. Labor tightness (actually DGS10 momentum) shows a real pullback in the 10-year toward a technical support level, but this is somewhat circular for a duration read since it's using DGS10 itself as an input — I discount it as it's not a fresh fundamental input, and it flags gaps versus balance-sheet/financial-conditions that other drivers cover better.

Net: balance-sheet runoff (accelerating, unanimous) and rising inflation dominate and pull the level up; NFCI easing and softening breakevens pull it down modestly. This is a genuine split versus last meeting — inflation conviction firmed further, balance-sheet runoff intensified rather than paused, while financial conditions genuinely eased (fresh 52-week low, not stale). I keep a small net-up duration tilt but trim conviction given the two-sided pull is now stronger than last meeting.

arbitrated.notes:
This panel looks confusingly labeled relative to last meeting — the names attached to reports have shifted (what was "term_premium" now looks like the balance-sheet/assets read, "balance_sheet" now reads like a curve-slope spread series, "labor_tightness" is actually describing DGS10 levels, and "inflation" is describing unemployment/Sahm-gap data). I'm reading through to the underlying substance rather than the labels, since the content is what matters for the level call.

Substantively: (1) Fed balance sheet continues to shrink (assets down again, 4wk and 13wk changes negative, pace decelerating but unbroken) — this is a persistent upward pressure on yields via reduced duration absorption. (2) The 10-year yield itself has been grinding higher across 21/63/252-day windows, near 12-month highs — that's already a price confirmation, not an independent driver, but it corroborates the direction. (3) Inflation expectations (breakevens, CPI, core PCE) have turned up: CPI y/y rising three straight months, core PCE ticking up, breakevens near top of range — a genuine reversal from my prior benign inflation read. (4) Financial conditions have flipped from my prior "easing/down" call toward "up" (breakevens bouncing, modest tightening lean), even as a separate NFCI-style series still shows conditions loosening — a genuine split within this bucket. (5) Labor/unemployment-type series shows plateau, not clearly loosening or tightening further.

Net: the previously stale, more dovish read (balance sheet down, financial conditions easing, inflation contained) has been overturned by fresher, consistent evidence — balance sheet still contracting, inflation momentum building, financial conditions leaning tighter. This argues for nudging the level view modestly higher in yields, extending rather than reversing my small existing long-yield tilt.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)

My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction for this print — the +0.20pp move was a real bounce, not noise, though it's within the recent 3.9-4.2% range that has held for many months. Looking at the full series, the rate has been oscillating in a tight 3.9-4.2% band with no clear trend for a year — 4.1, 4.2, 4.2, 4.1, 4.1, 4.2, 4.1, 4.0, 4.2. The 3m and 6m changes are literally 0.00pp, meaning this is choppy sideways movement rather than a sustained rise. The 12m change of +0.30pp and sahm_gap of +0.20pp show mild cumulative loosening from the low, but the level remains only modestly above the ~4% natural rate estimate. Policy language shifted to describing the market as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," which argues against reading this print as the start of a fresh deterioration trend — it looks more like normal month-to-month noise within a stable band. Given the range-bound history, I lean toward the next reading reverting toward the 4.0-4.1% area rather than extending the rise, but the recent jump raises some risk of continued upward drift.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive print above 4.2% would confirm a genuine break of the range and a real loosening trend rather than noise.

The trend is a steady grind lower, not a reversal-then-bounce; the most recent print (4.24) is the lowest in the series. Range position sits at 0.53, mid-range, so the level itself isn't extreme, but the recent momentum is clearly downward. The 30s10s slope at +0.27pp has not flattened to zero/negative, so the long-end curve isn't screaming distress, but it also isn't confirming renewed upward premium pressure. Balance-sheet runoff continues per the policy language, which is a structural upward force on term premium, but it has been overwhelmed by whatever is driving yields down recently (likely flight-to-quality or other cross-asset flows outside my coverage). Given the clear, sustained -0.31pp move over 21 days versus a flatter 63-day and 252-day change, I read this as a fresh downward move in the long end rather than noise. I'm revising to a lean-down call, with moderate conviction given the offsetting structural runoff pressure.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 4.40 with 30s10s steepening further would suggest the downward move was noise, not a new trend.

There is no sign of the two-consecutive-weekly-rise falsifier, and policy language remains unchanged: the Committee continues reducing Treasury, agency debt, and agency MBS holdings, with no mention of pausing or slowing caps. The pace over the most recent 4 weeks (~52B) is roughly in line with the 13-week average pace (~139B/13 ≈ 10.7B/week vs latest weekly drop of ~15.8B), suggesting no deceleration signal yet, though a single week's move can be noisy. Given the consistent trend, unchanged reinvestment policy language, and no evidence of any pause, the balance sheet is very likely to continue shrinking into next month-end. This remains a clear, low-ambiguity continuation of trend rather than an inflection.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises in fed_assets, or a statement announcing pause/end of runoff/reinvestment exceeding caps into growth.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has moved meaningfully against my prior "up" duration lean, and I need to respect that rather than defend a stale call. Note the naming confusion in this panel: there are two "balance_sheet"-labeled reports plus a "curve_slope" report and an "inflation_expectations" report layered over the original "inflation"/"labor_tightness" driver slots — I map by content, not label. The driver actually tracking Fed assets (the one titled "term_premium" here, reporting fed_assets data) shows an unbroken, undecelerated QT decline — clear, fresh, high-conviction continuation of balance-sheet shrinkage, which is a structural upward force on term premium/yields. The driver tracking the 10-year level itself (labeled "labor_tightness," discussing the 4.47→4.24 decline) reports yields falling sharply, a fresh signal not fully explained by the covered drivers — a real gap, since it cites flows "outside my coverage." The inflation-tracking driver (labeled "inflation_expectations," covering CPI/PCE) reversed hard from up to down: headline CPI decelerated, core PCE broke below its own flip threshold. That's a genuine, fresh reversal on my inflation view. The breakeven-tracking piece ("financial_conditions") also walked back its up-call to flat/mixed given the recent pullback and partial bounce. The genuine balance-sheet-proxy report (curve slope oriented, "balance_sheet") is choppy/inconclusive — near flat with a slight steepening residual. NFCI-based ("curve_slope") shows conditions still loose but modestly tightening off lows — a small hawkish nudge, low conviction.

Net: my prior modest "up" duration lean has lost its main support (inflation cooling reversed materially, breakevens stalled) while the balance-sheet drag (QT continuing, pushing yields up) is the one driver still firing cleanly. But the unexplained fresh drop in the 10Y itself is a real, live signal I can't ignore — it argues for trimming the long-standing modest long-yield lean rather than doubling down. I'll cut size significantly, keeping only a small residual tilt from continued QT, since inflation cooling and yield declines now dominate the fresher evidence.

Inflation reversal is one month of data; could be noise that re-accelerates next print.
Panel disagreement (up vs down vs flat across proxies) weakens conviction in either direction.

trade.rationale: Trim prior modest long-yield lean sharply: QT continuation still supports mild upward pressure, but cooling inflation and unexplained yield declines argue against holding a meaningful directional bet.

arbitrated.notes: Relative to last meeting, the panel is largely reaffirming its prior directions but with softer conviction and some genuine cross-currents. Balance sheet: assets keep falling (QT ongoing) but the redemption cap cut to $5B is a real, newly-confirmed deceleration — this remains a "down" measurement (own-driver sense) but its yield implication (less supply absorption pressure, capping term premium) is now corroborated independently by the curve_slope/term_premium analyst's own read, which also flagged the same QT-taper as premium-reducing. This convergence strengthens rather than weakens the prior call. Financial conditions (NFCI) has now moved decisively and persistently tighter across 4w/13w windows with accelerating momentum — this is a genuine turn from my prior near-flat read, and I should update: tightening financial conditions argue for higher, not lower, yields at the margin (or at least removes an easing tailwind). Inflation: headline CPI/PCE readings are genuinely mixed — headline fell sharply but core PCE ticked up and is running hot on a 3m annualized basis; net this is closer to flat-to-slightly-firmer than the clean disinflation I called last time, so I should temper the down-inflation conviction. Labor tightness (here proxied by long-end/10Y level dynamics per this panel's mapping) shows cooling that has stalled — a modest, low-conviction down lean only. Term premium continues to fall on QT continuation, but decelerating. Netting these: modest downward pull from slowing balance-sheet effects on term premium, offset by firmer financial conditions and stickier core inflation than before. This is a weaker, more balanced picture than my prior lean, arguing for keeping the position very small and roughly flat rather than building conviction either way. I hold the existing tiny long-yield tilt rather than resize meaningfully — no single driver here decisively turned enough to justify a bigger reversal, but the case for the down-yield lean has weakened relative to last meeting.

discounted.why: Stalled 21d trend and mid-range positioning give weak, low-conviction signal not worth resizing on.

arbitrated.falsifier: A confirmed reversal in NFCI back toward -0.48/-0.51, or a weekly rise in Fed assets, or core PCE breaking decisively above 3.1% would flip this toward a clearer up-yield call.

Panel is genuinely split between tightening financial conditions and easing supply technicals, weakening the level signal.
Core inflation stickiness could reassert upward pressure on yields if next print surprises hot.

trade.rationale: Panel signals are more balanced than last meeting — tightening financial conditions offset by decelerating QT and mixed inflation. Hold the small existing tilt rather than resize on thin, conflicting evidence.

The 3-month change (+0.20pp) and 12-month change (+0.30pp) both show mild upward drift, and the range position sits at 1.0 (currently at the top of the trailing range), but the 6-month change is only +0.10pp, indicating the pace of loosening has not accelerated—this looks like a plateau at the cycle high rather than a fresh breakout. My prior flat call was essentially correct: the rate did not drop back toward 4.0% nor did it break clearly above 4.2%, it simply held there. Policy language still describes conditions as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," and the shift to "risks roughly in balance" (from previous asymmetric framing) suggests the committee sees less urgency in labor slack, consistent with a plateau rather than acceleration. Given three consecutive flat readings at the high end of the range, with no confirmed break higher and no sign of reversal lower, the most defensible call remains flat, though the persistence at the 12-month high with sahm_gap of 0.30 keeps some modest upside risk in play.

The 30s10s slope at 0.49pp is modest and not signaling aggressive long-end steepening. The main new policy input — slowing the runoff pace (cutting the redemption cap from $25bn to $5bn) — reduces the pace of Treasury supply hitting the market via balance-sheet runoff, which is a modest disinflationary-for-term-premium force (less net supply pressure), reinforcing rather than reversing the recent downward drift. Waller's dissent preferring the faster runoff pace is a minor hawkish counter-note but doesn't change the operative policy. With the trend intact, no break above 4.40, and reduced runoff-driven supply pressure, I lean toward continued softness or stabilization in the term premium, though the pace of decline has decelerated (21d change is small), suggesting the move may be flattening out near current levels.

arbitrated.notes: The panel this month is a mix of relabeled drivers (curve_slope, inflation_expectations appear to be alternate cuts of financial_conditions and inflation) and the five core drivers I track. Reconciling: balance_sheet (fed assets still declining, -0.30 direction i.e. shrinking, but pace clearly decelerating under new $5B/$35B caps — this is yield-supportive via less supply pressure easing, but the "own measurement" of assets is still falling, so conviction stays negative but reduced). term_premium's own read (2s10s-style slope, up 0.30) shows continued steepening but momentum stalling near range highs — I read this as term premium modestly rising still, consistent with the long-end steepening pressure noted across labor_tightness's 30s10s reference. financial_conditions is genuinely split: the "up" call (breakeven-based, NFCI-style tightening reversal) conflicts with "curve_slope" down call (NFCI easing resuming). Given curve_slope's read is fresher and shows a clear 4-print easing streak with momentum, while financial_conditions' up call is a single-print wobble, I lean toward financial conditions loosening (down), but with low conviction given the split. Inflation: unemployment-based inflation proxy shows clear loosening (breaking to new 12m high), while the CPI-labeled inflation_expectations shows deceleration/flat. These conflict on direction — I weight the CPI-anchored read as more directly "inflation" and call it roughly flat-to-soft, discounting the unemployment-proxy as actually a labor read mislabeled. Labor_tightness itself (using DGS10-based description, oddly) shows yields elevated, flat-to-up, consistent with no strong reversal in term premium compression. Net effect on yield level: modest steepening pressure from term premium/balance-sheet deceleration is roughly offset by softening financial conditions and soft inflation trend. This nudges me only marginally toward keeping the tiny long position, not adding to it — the panel is genuinely split and mostly stale-refreshed without decisive net movement, so I hold current sizing rather than resize.

This panel has odd driver-label mismatches vs my seat (e.g., "labor_tightness" analyst is actually describing DGS10 level/momentum, "balance_sheet" analyst is describing 2s10s slope, "curve_slope" analyst is describing NFCI/financial conditions, "inflation_expectations" analyst is describing CPI/PCE, and "term_premium" analyst is describing Fed balance sheet/reserves). I'm reading through to the underlying substance rather than the labels, consistent with prior meetings.

Substance: (1) balance-sheet/QT — the "term_premium"-labeled report shows fed assets hitting a fresh series low, runoff accelerating, no sign of taper. This is a clear, non-stale down print on the balance sheet (own measurement falling), which is yield-supportive on term premium/duration supply grounds — pushes yields up. (2) Financial conditions (NFCI, reported under "curve_slope") continue to ease steadily to new 52-week lows — looser conditions, a modest yields-down force, fresh and consistent. (3) Inflation: mixed — headline CPI/PCE (labeled "inflation_expectations") reaccelerated notably (0.25% mom, yoy bounce), while the "inflation" seat (unemployment-labeled, stale 15 days) shows flat/range-bound. I weight the fresher, more specific CPI/PCE read higher: inflation trending modestly up. (4) Labor tightness (actually DGS10 level dynamics) shows the 10-year drifting to fresh local lows, a growth/flight-to-quality signal — this argues yields down, and conflicts with QT's upward pull. (5) The "balance_sheet"-labeled 2s10s slope data shows steepening momentum stalling — no strong level signal either way.

Net: modest easing in financial conditions and softer yield momentum are being offset by continued QT runoff and a real inflation uptick. This is close to my prior read but slightly more balanced — not enough new information to flip direction. I hold the position with slightly reduced conviction given the crosscurrents.

arbitrated.notes:
There's a naming confusion in this panel versus my seat framework: the analyst labeled "curve_slope" is actually reporting NFCI (financial conditions) data, and the analyst labeled "financial_conditions" is actually reporting breakeven/inflation-expectations data, while "labor_tightness" is actually reporting DGS10 level/term-premium-style data and "inflation" is reporting unemployment-rate data. I'm reading through the mislabeling to the underlying series each analyst actually cites, since that's what determines the yield-level implication, not the label on the box.

Substantively: balance_sheet (curve slope) shows genuine multi-print flattening — short end rising relative to long end — which is a modest curve signal but the analyst himself notes 10s30s isn't inverting, so it's not a strong net-level call on its own. True financial conditions (the NFCI-reporting one) show continued easing, at a 52-week low with no reversal — that's consistently disinflationary-adjacent for policy risk premia and argues for easier conditions capping yields, consistent with my prior read of -0.45. The true balance-sheet driver (Fed assets, still shrinking, -1.03% over 13 weeks) confirms QT continues unabated, a structural upward pressure on term premium/long-end yields — I keep this negative-on-its-own-measure per instructions (assets falling = negative headline) while its yield effect is upward. Labor tightness (actually unemployment-rate data) ticked up toward range highs, suggesting some labor softening — a decelerating economy signal, mildly yield-negative directionally. Inflation (CPI/PCE) continues a modest but real uptrend, not accelerating sharply, keeping some upward pressure on yields via inflation expectations. Net: the picture is little-changed from last meeting — financial conditions still easing (yield-neutral-to-negative), balance sheet still shrinking (yield-positive via term premium), inflation still mildly firming (yield-positive), labor softening a touch (yield-negative). These forces roughly offset as they did before. I see no driver that has "turned" decisively enough to flip my modest, low-conviction long-yields tilt.

The panel names here don't map cleanly onto my five mandated drivers, so I'm reading through: "balance_sheet" analyst is actually reporting on 2s10s slope (curve, not my seat), "term_premium" analyst is actually reporting Fed asset runoff (that's my balance_sheet driver), "financial_conditions" and "inflation_expectations" analysts are both on breakevens (inflation-adjacent, not NFCI), "curve_slope" analyst has the actual NFCI data (my financial_conditions driver), and "inflation"/"labor_tightness" analysts are both reporting on unemployment-rate-style series inverted with duration proxies — messy, but I'll map by content not by label.

Content-mapped: balance sheet (Fed assets) is still shrinking, unbroken and slightly accelerating — a clear, fresh, high-conviction call, unchanged from last meeting in direction, this is the one driver with real momentum and no staleness. Financial conditions (NFCI) have stalled at a very loose, 52-week-low plateau — no fresh easing, no reversal — this softens my prior "easing" read toward flat. Inflation signals are split: CPI y/y is plateauing at cycle high (mild up-lean, 16 days old, softer than before), while breakevens have also plateaued near range highs after a firm run — both lean modestly up but momentum has faded from last meeting's stronger prints. Labor-market-proxy driver (unrate-style) shows plateau at cycle high, modest up-lean. The 10y-level-and-slope-labeled driver ("labor_tightness" content) actually looks like duration/term-premium price action turning down over 21d/63d — a fresher, currently-in-motion signal that yields have been drifting lower recently, which is the freshest concrete price signal on offer.

Net: balance-sheet shrinkage remains the dominant upward force on yields (fresh, accelerating), partially offset by stalled-not-reversed easy financial conditions (mildly yield-negative bias fading) and inflation plateauing rather than breaking out. The recent price action itself (yields down 21d/63d) is the freshest read and argues for not adding duration-short conviction. arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting the panel has shifted modestly but coherently toward a slightly less-upward, more balanced read on yields. Balance_sheet — my prior anchor for upward pressure (-0.60 conviction, meaning shrinking assets pushing yields up) — has now gone to flat/choppy: the driver's own analyst confirms the steepening momentum that fed that call has stalled (21d negative, range oscillating 0.52-0.59), so I can no longer treat balance-sheet as a clean upward force. That's the single biggest change: the falsifier I set previously (weekly rise in assets / flattening runoff + NFCI back above -0.45) has partially triggered on the runoff-momentum side even though NFCI itself is pinned flat, not reversed. Term_premium is similarly walked back from "down" to "flat" — a real reversal in the reported direction, though the analyst still leans down given continuing QT guidance. Financial_conditions (breakevens) ticked down modestly — a soft, low-conviction easing signal, and flagged as potentially missing an inflation cross-read, so I discount its magnitude. Inflation remains the strongest, freshest signal: both CPI/PCE-inflation and the "loosening" labor-composite inflation proxy confirm continued upward drift, at meaningfully higher conviction than last time (0.45-0.55 vs 0.25 before). Labor tightness is flat/stalled, low conviction, no real information. Net: the previous case for modest higher yields (driven mainly by balance-sheet QT) has weakened, but the inflation impulse has actually gotten stronger and is the freshest, least-stale read in the room. This roughly offsets the loss of the balance-sheet impulse, leaving me still tilted mildly toward higher yields, but sized down given increased internal disagreement (balance_sheet and term_premium now flat vs inflation still up). I keep the position but trim conviction slightly rather than reverse it — no single driver has flipped hard enough against the standing trade to justify closing it.

arbitrated.notes: Relative to last meeting, the picture has shifted meaningfully on two fronts and stayed put on a third. First, note the labeling confusion in this panel: the analyst tagged "labor_tightness" is actually describing DGS10 level/curve dynamics and Fed balance-sheet policy (QT conclusion), not employment data — while "inflation" is actually reporting unemployment-rate dynamics (Sahm gap, unrate). I'm reading the substance, not the labels, and mapping each to what it actually measures. The genuine inflation-expectations read (breakevens stabilizing 2.28-2.30, flat, conviction 0.25) and the genuine inflation read (headline CPI accelerating to 3.02%, core PCE to 2.91%, conviction 0.55 up) together say price pressure is still drifting up, consistent with and slightly reinforcing my prior +0.50 call — I keep it near there. The unemployment-labeled report (rising to 4.40%, 12-month high) argues for a loosening labor market, which is disinflationary/dovish for yields — a new consideration I hadn't fully weighted last time. The term-premium/balance-sheet story is the biggest genuine change: the Fed has explicitly announced ending QT, a clear regime break from "continue reducing" to "conclude reduction." That removes an upward technical pressure on term premium/long-end supply, arguing for a bull-flattening lean on the long end even as the curve_slope and true balance_sheet analysts describe a rangebound 2s10s with only mild steepening bias. Financial conditions (both NFCI and breakevens-as-proxy) are genuinely flat/pinned, no impulse either way. Net: inflation still leans up, but the QT-conclusion news and rising unemployment reading both pull against higher yields, roughly offsetting the inflation pull. I'm trimming my long-duration-short (higher yield) position toward near-flat rather than reversing, since the panel is genuinely split and no single driver broke cleanly enough to flip the read outright.

Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

arbitrated.notes: The panel has shifted meaningfully softer versus last meeting, and I need to update. Note the naming confusion in this panel: the driver actually reporting on CPI/unemployment dynamics under the "inflation" label is describing unemployment rising to 12-month highs (labor loosening), while "labor_tightness" is actually reporting on DGS10 levels (a term-premium/duration proxy), and "financial_conditions" is reporting on breakevens (inflation expectations), with "curve_slope" reporting NFCI. I read through the mislabeling to the substance: true inflation expectations (breakevens, headline CPI y/y) are flat-to-softening — breakevens grinding lower to 2.23 near 12m lows, headline CPI y/y stalling at 2.99% off its highs, core PCE decelerating. That's a clear de-escalation from my prior +0.50 inflation conviction. Labor market (unemployment at 12m highs, Sahm gap near trigger) is loosening, which is disinflationary and dovish for yields — a reversal from my prior "tightening" read. Term premium: fed balance sheet still contracting on realized data (QT still running) even though language points to a halt — but the actual print curve (2s10s) has plateaued at 0.55 after touching 0.58, not breaking out, so no fresh steepening impulse. Financial conditions (NFCI) are flat, marginally tightening but not conviction-worthy. The confluence — softer inflation signals, loosening labor, stalled steepening, and QT still grinding — argues for a lower-yield lean, a real change from my prior modest-up stance. My falsifier conditions (CPI stall below 2.9%, QT follow-through, steepening break above 0.55) are partially met: CPI stalled near 2.9-3.0%, steepening capped at 0.55-0.58 without clean break. This tips me toward closing out the tiny long-yield tilt and moving to a slight downside lean, sized small given genuine cross-driver disagreement and staleness in a few reads.

2s10s plateau after failed breakout is genuinely flat — no directional signal to add.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI print back above 3.1% y/y, unemployment reversing below 4.30%, or fed assets stabilizing/rising would flip this back toward higher yields.

Panel disagreement (steepening stalled vs QT still running) means level signal is thin, position sized small on purpose.
If labor loosening reverses on a strong jobs print, yields could snap back up sharply given crowded dovish positioning.

trade.rationale: Softer inflation signals, loosening labor market, and continued realized QT drag tip the level view modestly lower versus my prior small long-yield tilt; sized down given cross-driver disagreement.
